Job

Wheel of Heaven Translation

The bnei-elohim council, the divine speeches, behemoth and leviathan

Direct Direct Established or uncontested across mainstream sources, or a direct description of the project itself. Framework Framework Foundational premise of the Wheel of Heaven framework. Explicit in the Raëlian canon; not endorsed by mainstream scholarship. Inferred Inferred A reasonable reading of comparative material — not stated literally, but consistent with the source. Speculative Speculative Interpretive synthesis or hypothesis that goes beyond what any single source states.

Wheel of Heaven Translation of Job from the pointed Hebrew Masoretic text (Westminster Leningrad Codex, Public Domain). Chapters 1–2 — the bnei-elohim council scenes and the two tests of Job — drafted 2026-05-22 against translation-glossary v2.5.0. The chapter's keystone passages: the *bnei ha-elohim* presenting themselves before YHWH (1:6, 2:1), with *ha-satan* among them as a council member functioning as adversarial-prosecutor (NOT yet the personified evil-deity of later Christian theology); Job's two responses (*YHWH gave, YHWH took*, 1:21; *shall we accept good and not accept bad*, 2:10). The book's keystone is the divine-council scene that the WoH lens reads in continuity with Gen 6:2-4 and Ps 82's *bnei-elohim* assemblies. Initial scope after 1-2: chapters 38–42 (the divine speeches with the *behemoth* and *leviathan* material). The Dhorme/RNKJV translation divergence the Raëlian canon notes (located in Job 37–38) is queued for the next translation pass.

Chapter 1

Job 1 — The bnei-elohim council and the first test

1
A man there was in the land of Uz, Job by name; and that man was blameless and upright, one who feared Elohim and turned from evil.
There was a man in the land of Uz, whose name was Job; and that man was perfect and upright, and one that feared God, and turned away from evil.
אִ֛ישׁ הָיָ֥ה בְאֶֽרֶץ־ע֖וּץ אִיּ֣וֹב שְׁמ֑וֹ וְהָיָ֣ה ׀ הָאִ֣ישׁ הַה֗וּא תָּ֧ם וְיָשָׁ֛ר וִירֵ֥א אֱלֹהִ֖ים וְסָ֥ר מֵרָֽע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The book opens outside Israel. Eretz Uz — the land of Uz — is identified in Gen 10:23 as descended from Aram (Shem's line), and in Gen 22:21 as Nahor's eldest son. The location is generally placed east of the Jordan, possibly in northern Arabia or Edom (Lam 4:21 locates 'the daughter of Edom dwelling in the land of Uz'). The chapter is staging Job's story outside Israel — among the Aramean / Edomite peripheries, in a non-Israelite cultural sphere. // Iyov (אִיּוֹב) — Job. The name's etymology is contested. Possible derivations: (1) from a root meaning the hated / persecuted one (cognate with Akkadian ayyabu — enemy); (2) from ayyeh-ab — where is the father; (3) an Amorite or West Semitic personal name with no obvious etymology. The chapter offers no folk etymology — unlike most Hebrew Bible introductions of named figures, Job's name is given without a wordplay. // Tam v'yashar — blameless and upright. Tam is from tom (completeness, integrity, wholeness — not 'perfect' in the moral-absolute sense but unblemished/whole in the ritual sense). Yashar (upright, straight) is the ethical-conduct twin. The pair recurs in 1:8, 2:3 as YHWH's own characterization of Job. // Yere Elohim v'sar me-ra — feared Elohim and turned from evil. The chapter is positioning Job in the Wisdom-literature register — yir'at Elohim (fear of Elohim) is the canonical Wisdom virtue (Prov 1:7, 9:10, 15:33). The chapter is announcing this is a Wisdom-book even before the dialogue starts.

2
Seven sons and three daughters were born to him.
And there were born unto him seven sons and three daughters.
וַיִּוָּ֥לְדוּ ל֛וֹ שִׁבְעָ֥ה בָנִ֖ים וְשָׁל֥וֹשׁ בָּנֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The numbers are symbolic. Seven sons + three daughters = ten children. Seven is the number of completeness; three is the number of formal-ritual structure; ten is the number of plenitude. The chapter is signaling Job's life as complete by the canonical symbolic numerology. The same numbers will recur at 42:13 in the restoration: seven sons, three daughters (the children Job is given after his ordeal). The book's symmetry-by-numbers is structural.

3
His livestock numbered seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of cattle, and five hundred donkeys, with very many servants — and that man was greater than all the sons of the east.
His substance also was seven thousand sheep, and three thousand camels, and five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she-asses, and a very great household; so that this man was the greatest of all the children of the east.
וַיְהִ֣י מִ֠קְנֵהוּ שִֽׁבְעַ֨ת אַלְפֵי־צֹ֜אן וּשְׁלֹ֧שֶׁת אַלְפֵ֣י גְמַלִּ֗ים וַחֲמֵ֨שׁ מֵא֤וֹת צֶֽמֶד־בָּקָר֙ וַחֲמֵ֣שׁ מֵא֣וֹת אֲתוֹנ֔וֹת וַעֲבֻדָּ֖ה רַבָּ֣ה מְאֹ֑ד וַיְהִי֙ הָאִ֣ישׁ הַה֔וּא גָּד֖וֹל מִכָּל־בְּנֵי־קֶֽדֶם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Job's wealth itemized. The numbers are pastoral-archetypal: seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels — the doubled seven/three pattern recurs. The chapter is establishing Job's wealth in the canonical Wisdom-narrative register: not a king (no land or political power named), but a pastoralist of the eastern peripheries. // Bnei qedem — sons of the east — names the eastern Aramean/Edomite pastoral peoples; Job is the greatest among them. The chapter is staging Job as a regional patriarch in the same sociological register as the Genesis patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) — though geographically east of the Jordan rather than in Canaan.

4
His sons used to go and hold a feast in each one's house on his day; and they would send and call for their three sisters to eat and drink with them.
And his sons went and held a feast in the house of each one upon his day; and they sent and called for their three sisters to eat and to drink with them.
וְהָלְכ֤וּ בָנָיו֙ וְעָשׂ֣וּ מִשְׁתֶּ֔ה בֵּ֖ית אִ֣ישׁ יוֹמ֑וֹ וְשָׁלְח֗וּ וְקָרְאוּ֙ לִשְׁל֣שֶׁת אַחְיֹתֵיהֶ֔ם לֶאֱכֹ֥ל וְלִשְׁתּ֖וֹת עִמָּהֶֽם׃
5
When the days of the feast had run their cycle, Job would send and consecrate them, rising early in the morning and offering whole burnt offerings according to the number of all of them — for Job said, "Perhaps my children have sinned and have blessed Elohim in their hearts." Thus Job did all the days.
And it was so, when the days of their feasting were gone about, that Job sent and sanctified them, and rose up early in the morning, and offered burnt-offerings according to the number of them all: for Job said, It may be that my sons have sinned, and renounced God in their hearts. Thus did Job continually.
וַיְהִ֡י כִּ֣י הִקִּיפֽוּ֩ יְמֵ֨י הַמִּשְׁתֶּ֜ה וַיִּשְׁלַ֧ח אִיּ֣וֹב וַֽיְקַדְּשֵׁ֗ם וְהִשְׁכִּ֣ים בַּבֹּקֶר֮ וְהֶעֱלָ֣ה עֹלוֹת֮ מִסְפַּ֣ר כֻּלָּם֒ כִּ֚י אָמַ֣ר אִיּ֔וֹב אוּלַי֙ חָטְא֣וּ בָנַ֔י וּבֵרֲכ֥וּ אֱלֹהִ֖ים בִּלְבָבָ֑ם כָּ֛כָה יַעֲשֶׂ֥ה אִיּ֖וֹב כָּל־הַיָּמִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Berakhu Elohim — literally blessed Elohim, but here the chapter is using the verb barakh as a euphemism for curse. The reverential-substitution device is well-attested in the Hebrew Bible: when the text needs to put curse-language next to Elohim or YHWH, it uses the opposite verb barakh (bless) instead. The substitution recurs in this chapter at 1:11 (the satan's prediction: 'he will bless you' = 'he will curse you to your face') and at 2:5, 2:9. The scribal-euphemistic device makes the meaning clear from context — Job is anxious that his children might have cursed Elohim, not that they blessed him. // The chapter is opening with Job's preemptive intercession on his children's behalf — he is a priestly-figure, offering olot (whole burnt offerings) for them, misper kullam (according to the number of all of them, individually). The verb kiddesh (sanctify, consecrate) is the cultic-purification action. Job functions as priest for his household, in a way that anticipates the Aaronic priesthood but precedes it textually.

6
The day came when the sons of the Elohim came to present themselves before YHWH — and the adversary also came among them.
Now it came to pass on the day when the sons of God came to present themselves before Jehovah, that Satan also came among them.
וַיְהִ֣י הַיּ֔וֹם וַיָּבֹ֙אוּ֙ בְּנֵ֣י הָאֱלֹהִ֔ים לְהִתְיַצֵּ֖ב עַל־יְהוָ֑ה וַיָּב֥וֹא גַֽם־הַשָּׂטָ֖ן בְּתוֹכָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The book's keystone scene. Vay'hi ha-yom — the day came — opens the heavenly-council scene. // Bnei ha-elohim — sons of the Elohim — the same plural-class divine-council figures named in Gen 6:2-4 and Ps 82:6, 89:6-7 (cf. glossary entry b-nei-ha-elohim). The chapter is staging a periodic council assembly: the bnei ha-elohim come l'hityatzev al-YHWH — to present themselves before YHWH. The verb yatzav in hithpael denotes formal-ritual presentation for inspection or duty (cf. Num 11:16 of the seventy elders, 1 Sam 10:19, 2 Chr 11:13). The bnei-ha-elohim are court members presenting themselves to their sovereign. // Ha-satan (הַשָּׂטָן) — the adversary. The Hebrew preserves the definite article ha — making this a functional role, not a proper name. Ha-satan is the satan, the adversary — a member of the divine council whose role is adversarial-prosecutorial. The same role-noun appears in 1 Chr 21:1 (where satan lacks the article and is read by some as a proper name), in Zech 3:1-2 (ha-satan standing at the right hand of the high priest Joshua to accuse him), and in Num 22:22, 32 (where YHWH's malakh stands as a satan — adversary — in Balaam's path). // The KJV/ASV rendering 'Satan' (capitalized, proper-noun) is a Christian-tradition retrojection. The figure of Satan-the-devil in later Christian theology is built up over centuries from disparate sources (the serpent of Gen 3, the morning-star of Isa 14, the dragon of Rev 12); the Hebrew Bible's ha-satan in Job 1-2 is a council-member with permission to test, not the personified evil-deity. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew article — 'the adversary' — matching the modern scholarly consensus (NJPS, Clines's Anchor Bible commentary, Habel's OTL, Newsom in NIB). The translation does not commit to the proper-name reading; the commentary surfaces the role-vs.-personified-deity question. // Va-yavo gam ha-satan b'tokham — and the adversary also came among them. The verse positions ha-satan as one of the bnei-ha-elohim — among them, presumably one of them. The chapter is staging adversarial-prosecution as a function internal to the divine council, not an external evil counterforce.

7
YHWH said to the adversary, "From where do you come?" And the adversary answered YHWH and said, "From roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it."
And Jehovah said unto Satan, Whence comest thou? Then Satan answered Jehovah, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it.
וַיֹּ֧אמֶר יְהוָ֛ה אֶל־הַשָּׂטָ֖ן מֵאַ֣יִן תָּבֹ֑א וַיַּ֨עַן הַשָּׂטָ֤ן אֶת־יְהוָה֙ וַיֹּאמַ֔ר מִשּׁ֣וּט בָּאָ֔רֶץ וּמֵֽהִתְהַלֵּ֖ךְ בָּֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

Shut ba-aretz u-me-hithallekh bah — from roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it. Two verbs of locomotion: shut (rove, range) and hithallekh (walk-about). // The hithpael mithallekh is the chapter's most pointed lexical echo. The same verbal form is used of YHWH-Elohim walking about in the garden (Gen 3:8), of Enoch walking about with the Elohim (Gen 5:22, 24), of Noah walking about with the Elohim (Gen 6:9). The verb denotes habitual, at-home movement-in-residence. // Ha-satan walks-about on the earth — in the same verbal form as the patriarchs walked-about with the Elohim. The chapter is positioning ha-satan as having the earth as its movement-domain in the same way the antediluvian-favored figures had divine company as theirs. The role is territorial-investigative: ha-satan ranges the earth, presumably collecting information for the council. The function is prosecutorial — an inspector or examiner — not metaphysically-evil. // The chapter does not give ha-satan an explicit job-description. The Hebrew Bible elsewhere implies the role: in Zech 3 ha-satan stands as legal accuser; in Num 22 the malakh-as-satan blocks Balaam. Ha-satan in Job 1-2 ranges the earth, comes to council, and is queried about its findings. The function-set is adversarial-prosecutor-and-inspector.

8
YHWH said to the adversary, "Have you set your heart on my servant Job? For there is none like him on the earth — a man blameless and upright, one who fears Elohim and turns from evil."
And Jehovah said unto Satan, Hast thou considered my servant Job? for there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and turneth away from evil.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ אֶל־הַשָּׂטָ֔ן הֲשַׂ֥מְתָּ לִבְּךָ֖ עַל־עַבְדִּ֣י אִיּ֑וֹב כִּ֣י אֵ֤ין כָּמֹ֙הוּ֙ בָּאָ֔רֶץ אִ֣ישׁ תָּ֧ם וְיָשָׁ֛ר יְרֵ֥א אֱלֹהִ֖ים וְסָ֥ר מֵרָֽע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

YHWH initiates the topic of Job. Hasamta libekha al-avdi Iyov — have you set your heart on my servant Job? The phrase sim lev al (set heart upon) means give attention to, focus on, scrutinize. YHWH is asking whether ha-satan has noticed Job specifically. // Avdi — my servant — is the canonical Hebrew Bible designation for figures of singular divine-relational status: Moses (avdi Moshe, Num 12:7-8, Deut 34:5, Josh 1:2), David (avdi David, 2 Sam 7:5, 1 Kgs 11:13), the Isaianic Suffering Servant (Isa 52:13). Job is now named in the same register. The chapter is positioning Job as a figure of high covenantal status — YHWH's servant, of singular-exemplary character. // YHWH's recitation of Job's characterization — tam v'yashar yere Elohim v'sar me-ra — is identical to the narrator's at 1:1. The divine voice and the narrator's voice agree: Job is what he is said to be. // The chapter is structurally setting up the test: YHWH points out Job; ha-satan will challenge the basis of Job's righteousness. The challenge is not over Job's piety (no one disputes that) but over its motivation (transactional vs. unconditional).

9
The adversary answered YHWH and said, "Does Job fear Elohim for nothing?"
Then Satan answered Jehovah, and said, Doth Job fear God for nought?
וַיַּ֧עַן הַשָּׂטָ֛ן אֶת־יְהוָ֖ה וַיֹּאמַ֑ר הַֽחִנָּ֔ם יָרֵ֥א אִיּ֖וֹב אֱלֹהִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The adversary's challenge. Ha-chinnam yare Iyov Elohim — does Job fear Elohim for nothing / freely? The crucial word is chinnam (חִנָּם) — for nothing, gratis, without compensation, without cause. The adverb appears in Gen 29:15 (Laban: 'should you serve me for nothing?'), Ex 21:2 (the freed slave goes out chinnam, without payment), Num 11:5 (the fish 'we ate in Egypt chinnam' — without cost). // Ha-satan's challenge is precisely-targeted: the question is not whether Job is righteous but whether his righteousness is transactional. The accusation: Job worships because Job is paid to (with wealth, children, security). Remove the payment — the argument runs — and the worship collapses. // The argument is technically interesting. It assumes that worship-without-reward would be the genuine article, and worship-with-reward is merely paid behavior. The chapter is staging the question of motivation as the test. // Chinnam will return through the book as a keyword. Job himself in 2:3 will be called gratuitous — t'sitheni vo l'val'o chinnam — you incited me against him to swallow him without cause.

10
Have you not put a hedge around him and around his house and around all that is his on every side? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his livestock has spread out in the land.
Hast not thou made a hedge about him, and about his house, and about all that he hath, on every side? thou hast blessed the work of his hands, and his substance is increased in the land.
הֲלֹֽא־את [אַ֠תָּה] שַׂ֣כְתָּ בַעֲד֧וֹ וּבְעַד־בֵּית֛וֹ וּבְעַ֥ד כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־ל֖וֹ מִסָּבִ֑יב מַעֲשֵׂ֤ה יָדָיו֙ בֵּרַ֔כְתָּ וּמִקְנֵ֖הוּ פָּרַ֥ץ בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The adversary's evidence. Ha-satan lays out the case: YHWH has hedged Job in (sakhta ba'ado — the verb sakhakh covers enclose, screen, protect). The hedge is comprehensive: around Job, around his house, around everything he has. YHWH has blessed (berakhta) the work of Job's hands and spread out (paratz) his livestock. // The verse reads as a divine cost-benefit summary: YHWH has invested heavily in Job's prosperity. The adversary's implicit argument: no wonder he's pious — look at the deal you've given him. // Ketiv/qere note: the text has at (אַת — second-person feminine singular pronoun) as ketiv; the masoretic vocalization is attah (אַתָּה — second-person masculine singular) as qere. The chapter accepts the qere reading addressed to YHWH.

11
But put out your hand and touch all that is his — and surely he will curse you to your face.
But put forth thy hand now, and touch all that he hath, and he will renounce thee to thy face.
וְאוּלָם֙ שְֽׁלַֽח־נָ֣א יָֽדְךָ֔ וְגַ֖ע בְּכָל־אֲשֶׁר־ל֑וֹ אִם־לֹ֥א עַל־פָּנֶ֖יךָ יְבָרֲכֶֽךָּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The proposed test. Shelach-na yadekha v'ga b'khol-asher-lo — put out your hand and touch all that is his. Naga (touch) is the chapter's understatement-verb for the catastrophic disasters of vv 13-19 — to touch is to bring devastating loss. // Im-lo al-panekha y'varakhekka — literally if not to your face he will bless you. The Hebrew uses the strong oath-formula im-lo — equivalent to surely — and the verb barakh (bless) as the euphemism for curse (cf. commentary at 1:5). The adversary's prediction in plain language: he will curse you to your face. // The chapter is restating its central wager precisely. The adversary's case: remove the hedge, and the worship reverses. YHWH's confidence: Job's piety is unconditional.

12
YHWH said to the adversary, "Behold, all that he has is in your hand — only against him do not put out your hand." And the adversary went out from before YHWH.
And Jehovah said unto Satan, Behold, all that he hath is in thy power; only upon himself put not forth thy hand. So Satan went forth from the presence of Jehovah.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־הַשָּׂטָ֗ן הִנֵּ֤ה כָל־אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ֙ בְּיָדֶ֔ךָ רַ֣ק אֵלָ֔יו אַל־תִּשְׁלַ֖ח יָדֶ֑ךָ וַיֵּצֵא֙ הַשָּׂטָ֔ן מֵעִ֖ם פְּנֵ֥י יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The permission and its limit. Kol asher-lo b'yadkha — all that he has is in your hand. The adversary is granted delegated authority over Job's possessions and household. The limit: raq elav al-tishlach yadkha — only against him do not put out your hand — Job himself is exempt from the first test. // The chapter is making the structure of authority explicit: ha-satan acts under YHWH's permission, within boundaries YHWH sets. This is the strongest textual datum against the later Christian Satan-the-devil reading: ha-satan in Job 1 is neither autonomous nor opposed to YHWH; ha-satan operates within YHWH's delegated authority, bound by YHWH's specified limits. The function is adversarial (testing, accusing) but not adversarial-to-YHWH. // Va-yetze ha-satan me-im pnei YHWH — and the adversary went out from before YHWH. The council scene ends; the test begins.

13
The day came when his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the house of their oldest brother;
And it fell on a day when his sons and his daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother`s house,
וַיְהִ֖י הַיּ֑וֹם וּבָנָ֨יו וּבְנֹתָ֤יו אֹֽכְלִים֙ וְשֹׁתִ֣ים יַ֔יִן בְּבֵ֖ית אֲחִיהֶ֥ם הַבְּכֽוֹר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The disaster sequence begins. Vay'hi ha-yom — the day came — the same chapter-formula as 1:6 (the council scene). The chapter is staging the disasters on a day that the family was celebrating — making the loss specific and dated. // The next four verses (vv 14-19) form a tight quartet: four disasters reported in four parallel-structured messenger-arrival sequences, with the same closing formula va-imal'tah raq ani l'vadi l'haggid lakh — and I alone have escaped to tell you.

14
A messenger came to Job and said, "The oxen were plowing and the donkeys were grazing beside them,
that there came a messenger unto Job, and said, The oxen were plowing, and the asses feeding beside them;
וּמַלְאָ֛ךְ בָּ֥א אֶל־אִיּ֖וֹב וַיֹּאמַ֑ר הַבָּקָר֙ הָי֣וּ חֹֽרְשׁ֔וֹת וְהָאֲתֹנ֖וֹת רֹע֥וֹת עַל־יְדֵיהֶֽם׃
15
when Sheba fell upon them and took them, and they struck down the young men with the edge of the sword. I alone have escaped to tell you."
and the Sabeans fell [upon them], and took them away: yea, they have slain the servants with the edge of the sword; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
וַתִּפֹּ֤ל שְׁבָא֙ וַתִּקָּחֵ֔ם וְאֶת־הַנְּעָרִ֖ים הִכּ֣וּ לְפִי־חָ֑רֶב וָֽאִמָּ֨לְטָ֧ה רַק־אֲנִ֛י לְבַדִּ֖י לְהַגִּ֥יד לָֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

First disaster: Sheba raiders. Sheba (שְׁבָא) — the southern Arabian kingdom (Saba) named in Gen 10:7 (Cushite branch) and Gen 10:28 (Joktanite branch). The chapter is reporting a southern-Arabian raid on Job's livestock. The first disaster is human-causal — a raid by a named people.

16
While this one was still speaking, another came and said, "Fire of Elohim fell from the skies and burned up the sheep and the young men and consumed them. I alone have escaped to tell you."
While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, The fire of God is fallen from heaven, and hath burned up the sheep and the servants, and consumed them; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
ע֣וֹד ׀ זֶ֣ה מְדַבֵּ֗ר וְזֶה֮ בָּ֣א וַיֹּאמַר֒ אֵ֣שׁ אֱלֹהִ֗ים נָֽפְלָה֙ מִן־הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וַתִּבְעַ֥ר בַּצֹּ֛אן וּבַנְּעָרִ֖ים וַתֹּאכְלֵ֑ם וָאִמָּ֨לְטָ֧ה רַק־אֲנִ֛י לְבַדִּ֖י לְהַגִּ֥יד לָֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Second disaster: esh Elohim from the skies. Esh Elohim nafelah min ha-shamayim — fire of Elohim fell from the skies. The expression is striking. Esh Elohim (fire of Elohim) is a recurring Hebrew Bible designation for divinely-sent fire — lightning, supernatural conflagration. The same phrase appears at 2 Kgs 1:12 (the fire that consumes the captains of fifties before Elijah). // The lens reads the verse with attention: fire from the skies (literal lightning or atmospheric-electrical phenomenon) burned the flock and shepherds. The chapter is naming a meteorological-disaster in technical-cosmological vocabulary. // The disasters are escalating in agent-type: first human (Sheba raiders), now sky-fire. The pattern will continue: a third human-attack (Kasdim raiders, v17), then a wind-disaster (v19).

17
While this one was still speaking, another came and said, "The Kasdim formed three columns and raided the camels and took them, and they struck down the young men with the edge of the sword. I alone have escaped to tell you."
While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, The Chaldeans made three bands, and fell upon the camels, and have taken them away, yea, and slain the servants with the edge of the sword; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
ע֣וֹד ׀ זֶ֣ה מְדַבֵּ֗ר וְזֶה֮ בָּ֣א וַיֹּאמַר֒ כַּשְׂדִּ֞ים שָׂ֣מוּ ׀ שְׁלֹשָׁ֣ה רָאשִׁ֗ים וַֽיִּפְשְׁט֤וּ עַל־הַגְּמַלִּים֙ וַיִּקָּח֔וּם וְאֶת־הַנְּעָרִ֖ים הִכּ֣וּ לְפִי־חָ֑רֶב וָאִמָּ֨לְטָ֧ה רַק־אֲנִ֛י לְבַדִּ֖י לְהַגִּ֥יד לָֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Third disaster: Kasdim raiders. Kasdim — same people as named in Gen 11:28 (Ur Kasdim) and Ezek 1:3 (eretz Kasdim) — the Aramean-tribal people who became the Neo-Babylonian dynasty in the first millennium BCE. In Job 1:17 they are raiders, not yet imperial. The shloshah rashim (three columns) is military-tactical vocabulary — a coordinated raid in three contingents. // The book is set early (the patriarchal-archaic register the chapter has established at v3); the Kasdim appearance is anachronistic with that early setting but consistent with the chapter's broader chronological-mixing (cf. Ur Kasdim in Gen 11). The chapter is treating the Kasdim as a generic raiding-Aramean people without committing to a specific historical period.

18
While this one was still speaking, another came and said, "Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the house of their oldest brother,
While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, Thy sons and thy daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother`s house;
עַ֚ד זֶ֣ה מְדַבֵּ֔ר וְזֶ֖ה בָּ֣א וַיֹּאמַ֑ר בָּנֶ֨יךָ וּבְנוֹתֶ֤יךָ אֹֽכְלִים֙ וְשֹׁתִ֣ים יַ֔יִן בְּבֵ֖ית אֲחִיהֶ֥ם הַבְּכֽוֹר׃
19
and behold, a great wind came from beyond the desert and struck the four corners of the house — and it fell on the young people, and they died. I alone have escaped to tell you."
and, behold, there came a great wind from the wilderness, and smote the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they are dead; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
וְהִנֵּה֩ ר֨וּחַ גְּדוֹלָ֜ה בָּ֣אָה ׀ מֵעֵ֣בֶר הַמִּדְבָּ֗ר וַיִּגַּע֙ בְּאַרְבַּע֙ פִּנּ֣וֹת הַבַּ֔יִת וַיִּפֹּ֥ל עַל־הַנְּעָרִ֖ים וַיָּמ֑וּתוּ וָאִמָּ֨לְטָ֧ה רַק־אֲנִ֛י לְבַדִּ֖י לְהַגִּ֥יד לָֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

Fourth disaster: a great wind from beyond the desert. Ruach gedolah ba'ah me-ever ha-midbar — a great wind came from beyond the desert. The verb naga (touch, strike) names the wind's action — it struck the four corners of the house. // The wind is described as a single-event localized phenomenon: it came from across the desert, struck the house at all four corners simultaneously, brought the house down on the occupants. The combination of coming from beyond the desert, striking simultaneously at four points, and causing total structural collapse fits a strong wind-event (possibly a downburst or strong khamsin-style desert windstorm). // The four disasters together: Sheba raid, sky-fire, Kasdim raid, desert windstorm. Two human (1, 3), two natural (2, 4) — but the natural ones are described in divine-causal vocabulary (esh Elohim and ruach gedolah). The chapter is staging the disasters as having both human and natural-meteorological agents, with all four under ha-satan's delegated authority.

20
Job arose, tore his robe, sheared his head, fell to the ground, and worshipped.
Then Job arose, and rent his robe, and shaved his head, and fell down upon the ground, and worshipped;
וַיָּ֤קָם אִיּוֹב֙ וַיִּקְרַ֣ע אֶת־מְעִל֔וֹ וַיָּ֖גָז אֶת־רֹאשׁ֑וֹ וַיִּפֹּ֥ל אַ֖רְצָה וַיִּשְׁתָּֽחוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Three mourning-actions, then worship. Qara me'il (tore his robe), gazaz rosh (sheared/shaved his head), nafal artzah (fell to the ground). These are the standard Hebrew Bible mourning rites (cf. Gen 37:34, 2 Sam 13:31, Ezra 9:3, Mic 1:16). Then yishtachu — worshipped — the standard verb for prostration-in-worship. // The verb-sequence is precise. Job mourns by the standard rites, but then worships. He does what one does when one has lost everything — and then orients toward YHWH. The chapter is staging the response that vv 21-22 will explicitly name: not curse but blessing-in-the-strict-sense.

21
And he said, "Naked I came forth from my mother's womb, and naked I shall return there. YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken; may the name of YHWH be blessed."
and he said, Naked came I out of my mother`s womb, and naked shall I return thither: Jehovah gave, and Jehovah hath taken away; blessed be the name of Jehovah.
וַיֹּאמֶר֩ עָרֹ֨ם יצתי [יָצָ֜אתִי] מִבֶּ֣טֶן אִמִּ֗י וְעָרֹם֙ אָשׁ֣וּב שָׁ֔מָה יְהוָ֣ה נָתַ֔ן וַֽיהוָ֖ה לָקָ֑ח יְהִ֛י שֵׁ֥ם יְהוָ֖ה מְבֹרָֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The book's most-quoted line. YHWH natan v'YHWH laqach — y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh — YHWH gave and YHWH has taken — may the name of YHWH be blessed. The two clauses are perfectly balanced: the same verb-class (natan — give, laqach — take), the same divine subject (YHWH), the opposite actions. // The chapter's keystone theological-claim: Job acknowledges that the same agent who gave now takes. The acknowledgment is unconditional — neither the giving nor the taking changes Job's orientation. The response y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh — may the name of YHWH be blessed — uses barakh in its strict-positive sense (here genuinely blessed, not the euphemism-for-curse of v5, v11). The verse is the precise inverse of the adversary's prediction (v11): the adversary expected Job to curse (using barakh as euphemism); Job actually blesses (using barakh in its strict sense). // Arom mi-beten immi — naked from my mother's womb. The naked-came-naked-go formula picks up the universal-mortality acknowledgment (cf. Eccl 5:14 — 'naked he came from his mother's womb, and naked he shall return as he came'). The chapter is invoking the common-human-condition register: Job does not claim special-Job-status; he speaks as a human who came-and-will-go in the universal condition. // Ketiv/qere note: yatsati (qere) for yatsa-ti (ketiv) — the personal-affixed form vs. the simpler form.

22
In all this Job did not sin, nor did he charge Elohim with anything unseemly.
In all this Job sinned not, nor charged God foolishly.
בְּכָל־זֹ֖את לֹא־חָטָ֣א אִיּ֑וֹב וְלֹא־נָתַ֥ן תִּפְלָ֖ה לֵאלֹהִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The first test's verdict. Lo chata Iyov v'lo natan tiflah l'Elohim — Job did not sin, nor did he charge Elohim with anything unseemly. // The verb chata (sin, miss-the-mark) is in its standard cultic-ethical register. Tiflah (תִּפְלָה) is a hapax-style noun from a root tafel (unsavoury, insipid, foolish) — denoting something distasteful or something improperly directed. Job did not put unsavoury speech against Elohim. // The verse is the chapter's narrator-judgment: Job passed the first test. The verse closes the chapter; the next chapter will open with a second council scene and a second, escalated test. The adversary's wager has lost round one.

Chapter 2

Job 2 — The second council scene and the second test

1
The day came when the sons of the Elohim came to present themselves before YHWH — and the adversary also came among them to present himself before YHWH.
Again it came to pass on the day when the sons of God came to present themselves before Jehovah, that Satan came also among them to present himself before Jehovah.
וַיְהִ֣י הַיּ֔וֹם וַיָּבֹ֙אוּ֙ בְּנֵ֣י הָֽאֱלֹהִ֔ים לְהִתְיַצֵּ֖ב עַל־יְהוָ֑ה וַיָּב֤וֹא גַֽם־הַשָּׂטָן֙ בְּתֹכָ֔ם לְהִתְיַצֵּ֖ב עַל־יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The second council scene opens. The verse is nearly identical to 1:6 — same opening formula (vay'hi ha-yom), same convocation (bnei ha-elohim... l'hityatzev al-YHWH), same arrival (va-yavo gam ha-satan b'tokham). The chapter is staging the council as a periodic recurrence — these gatherings happen on the day, repeatedly. // One small addition: 2:1 adds l'hityatzev al-YHWH to ha-satan's arrival as well — making explicit that ha-satan presents himself in the same posture as the rest of the bnei ha-elohim. The chapter is reinforcing the role-internal-to-council framing of chapter 1.

2
YHWH said to the adversary, "From where do you come?" And the adversary answered YHWH and said, "From roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it."
And Jehovah said unto Satan, From whence comest thou? And Satan answered Jehovah, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ אֶל־הַשָּׂטָ֔ן אֵ֥י מִזֶּ֖ה תָּבֹ֑א וַיַּ֨עַן הַשָּׂטָ֤ן אֶת־יְהוָה֙ וַיֹּאמַ֔ר מִשֻּׁ֣ט בָּאָ֔רֶץ וּמֵהִתְהַלֵּ֖ךְ בָּֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The same opening exchange. YHWH's question and ha-satan's response are nearly verbatim from 1:7. The chapter is staging the council as a repeatable scene — the same opening protocol, the same response. The narrative is signaling that ha-satan's range-the-earth-and-report function is its standing-role, not a one-time investigation.

3
YHWH said to the adversary, "Have you set your heart on my servant Job? For there is none like him on the earth — a man blameless and upright, one who fears Elohim and turns from evil. He still holds firm to his integrity, and you incited me against him to swallow him without cause."
And Jehovah said unto Satan, Hast thou considered my servant Job? for there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and turneth away from evil: and he still holdeth fast his integrity, although thou movedst me against him, to destroy him without cause.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־הַשָּׂטָ֗ן הֲשַׂ֣מְתָּ לִבְּךָ֮ אֶל־עַבְדִּ֣י אִיּוֹב֒ כִּי֩ אֵ֨ין כָּמֹ֤הוּ בָּאָ֙רֶץ֙ אִ֣ישׁ תָּ֧ם וְיָשָׁ֛ר יְרֵ֥א אֱלֹהִ֖ים וְסָ֣ר מֵרָ֑ע וְעֹדֶ֙נּוּ֙ מַחֲזִ֣יק בְּתֻמָּת֔וֹ וַתְּסִיתֵ֥נִי ב֖וֹ לְבַלְּע֥וֹ חִנָּֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The verdict on round one — and the term 'incited'. YHWH reaffirms Job's characterization (the same five-fold formula from 1:1 and 1:8), then notes the persistence: odennu machaziq b'tumato — he still holds firm to his integrity. Tumah is the noun-form of tam — integrity, completeness. Job has maintained his tom-v-yashar status through the first test. // Va-tesiteni vo l'val'o chinnam — and you incited me against him to swallow him without cause. Two notable verbs. // First, vatesiteni — you incited me — from the verb sut (incite, instigate, provoke). The verb is also used of the lying spirit in 1 Kgs 22:20-22 (incite Ahab) and of Saul's persecution of David (1 Sam 26:19). YHWH is acknowledging that the test was at ha-satan's initiative — YHWH was prompted into it. The chapter is staging the divine response as responding to ha-satan's challenge rather than originating it. // Second, chinnam (the same word as 1:9) — without cause / for nothing. The chapter is doing wordplay: ha-satan accused Job of being righteous chinnam (without compensation, transactional); YHWH now describes the test as having been undertaken chinnam (without cause, gratuitously). The accusation has been reversed: the adversary is the one who acted without cause. // The verb l'val'o (to swallow him) is the strongest possible language for total destruction. YHWH is naming the test as l'val'o — a swallowing-up — which makes the first test's escalation explicit.

4
The adversary answered YHWH and said, "Skin for skin — all that a person has, he will give for his life."
And Satan answered Jehovah, and said, Skin for skin, yea, all that a man hath will he give for his life.
וַיַּ֧עַן הַשָּׂטָ֛ן אֶת־יְהוָ֖ה וַיֹּאמַ֑ר ע֣וֹר בְּעַד־ע֗וֹר וְכֹל֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר לָאִ֔ישׁ יִתֵּ֖ן בְּעַ֥ד נַפְשֽׁוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The adversary's second argument: skin for skin. Or be'ad or is a proverb-form. The Hebrew preposition be'ad (for, on behalf of, in exchange for) sets up an exchange-relationship. Two readings have circulated: // (1) Self-preservation reading. Skin (Job's body) is what he'll give in exchange for his life — i.e., he'll sacrifice anything up to his own bodily integrity to preserve his life. The argument: Job has weathered the loss of family and property, but he hasn't yet had to give up himself. // (2) Substitution reading. One sheds the skin of others (his children, his servants) to save one's own skin — i.e., Job tolerated the first test because the losses were external; he'll break when the test reaches his own body. // Both readings converge on the same prediction: the next test must reach Job's body. The chapter's argument-structure is escalating from what Job has to what Job is. // The proverb or be'ad or has not been securely identified in any other ANE source. The construction is unique in the Hebrew Bible. The chapter is staging the adversary as using folk-wisdom that the audience would recognize.

5
But put out your hand and touch his bone and his flesh — and surely he will curse you to your face.
But put forth thy hand now, and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will renounce thee to thy face.
אוּלָם֙ שְֽׁלַֽח־נָ֣א יָֽדְךָ֔ וְגַ֥ע אֶל־עַצְמ֖וֹ וְאֶל־בְּשָׂר֑וֹ אִם־לֹ֥א אֶל־פָּנֶ֖יךָ יְבָרֲכֶֽךָּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The proposed second test. The adversary's prediction is identical to 1:11 in its closing formula (al-panekha y'varakhekka — to your face he will curse you, using barakh as euphemism for curse — see commentary at 1:5). // The proposed test-action: ga el atzmo v'el b'saro — touch his bone and his flesh. The chapter is specifying the bodily test, where Job himself becomes the target. Etzem (bone) and basar (flesh) are the same vocabulary cluster as Ezekiel 37's staged-construction sequence (gidim → basar → `or → ruach); here they are the dimensions of Job's bodily integrity to be reached.

6
YHWH said to the adversary, "Behold, he is in your hand — only spare his life."
And Jehovah said unto Satan, Behold, he is in thy hand; only spare his life.
וַיֹּ֧אמֶר יְהוָ֛ה אֶל־הַשָּׂטָ֖ן הִנּ֣וֹ בְיָדֶ֑ךָ אַ֖ךְ אֶת־נַפְשׁ֥וֹ שְׁמֹֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The second permission and its new limit. Hinno b'yadkha — behold, he is in your hand. The limit is moved: in 1:12 the limit was Job himself; now Job himself is included, with only nafsho (his life-soul, his life-as-such) excluded. The escalation is precise — what was protected becomes available, what remains protected becomes the irreducible. // Et nafsho shmor — only spare his life. The verb shamar (guard, watch over, preserve) makes ha-satan responsible for not killing Job. The boundary is: Job's bodily integrity may be attacked, his life must be preserved.

7
The adversary went out from before YHWH and struck Job with severe sores from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head.
So Satan went forth from the presence of Jehovah, and smote Job with sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown.
וַיֵּצֵא֙ הַשָּׂטָ֔ן מֵאֵ֖ת פְּנֵ֣י יְהוָ֑ה וַיַּ֤ךְ אֶת־אִיּוֹב֙ בִּשְׁחִ֣ין רָ֔ע מִכַּ֥ף רַגְל֖וֹ ועד [וְעַ֥ד] קָדְקֳדֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The bodily affliction. Shechin ra — severe sores — describes a comprehensive skin-disease covering Job from sole to scalp. Shechin (שְׁחִין) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for severe inflammatory skin afflictions; it appears at Ex 9:9-11 (the sixth Egyptian plague), Lev 13:18-23 (Levitical priestly diagnosis of skin disease), Deut 28:27, 35, 2 Kgs 20:7 (Hezekiah's affliction). The exact dermatological identification is debated (smallpox, severe boils, leprosy in the old sense, an infected eruption); the chapter does not specify the medical-diagnostic detail. // Mi-kaf raglo v'ad qodqodo — from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head — the standard Hebrew Bible totality-formula (cf. Deut 28:35, 2 Sam 14:25, Isa 1:6) — the affliction is bodily-comprehensive. // Ketiv/qere note: v'ad (qere) for u'ad (ketiv) — minor preposition variant; the qere reading is adopted.

8
He took a potsherd to scrape himself with, and he sat in the midst of the ashes.
And he took him a potsherd to scrape himself therewith; and he sat among the ashes.
וַיִּֽקַּֽח־ל֣וֹ חֶ֔רֶשׂ לְהִתְגָּרֵ֖ד בּ֑וֹ וְה֖וּא יֹשֵׁ֥ב בְּתוֹךְ־הָאֵֽפֶר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Two iconic details. Cheres l'hithgared bo — a potsherd to scrape himself with. The verb garad (scrape, scratch) names the action; cheres is a broken pottery shard. The image is one of the Hebrew Bible's most-cited iconographies: the suffering-figure scraping his sores with a piece of broken pottery. // V'hu yoshev b'tokh ha-efer — and he sat in the midst of the ashes. Efer (ashes, dust) is the standard Hebrew Bible substance of mourning (cf. Esth 4:1, 4:3 of Mordecai; Jer 6:26 of mourning generally; Job 42:6 of Job's eventual self-abasement). The chapter is establishing the mourning-posture: stripped of property, family, and now bodily integrity, Job sits in ashes scraping at his afflicted skin. // Both details — the potsherd and the ashes — become the iconographic signature of Job in art and exegesis. The chapter is staging the lowest possible posture for a wealthy patriarch.

9
His wife said to him, "Are you still holding firm to your integrity? Curse Elohim — and die."
Then said his wife unto him, Dost thou still hold fast thine integrity? renounce God, and die.
וַתֹּ֤אמֶר לוֹ֙ אִשְׁתּ֔וֹ עֹדְךָ֖ מַחֲזִ֣יק בְּתֻמָּתֶ֑ךָ בָּרֵ֥ךְ אֱלֹהִ֖ים וָמֻֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The wife's challenge. Odekha machaziq b'tumatekha — are you still holding firm to your integrity? The wife is using the same verb YHWH used in 2:3 (odennu machaziq b'tumato — he still holds firm to his integrity). The chapter is staging her as having direct insight into the divine assessment — though she draws the opposite conclusion. // Barekh Elohim va-mut — literally bless Elohim and die, but barekh is again the euphemism for curse (the third occurrence of this scribal device in the book: cf. 1:5, 1:11, 2:5; commentary at 1:5 for the euphemism). The wife's plain instruction: curse Elohim and die. // The wife has been read at every level — as faithless tempter (the patristic reading), as compassionate-deliverer (suggesting Job blaspheme to provoke YHWH's killing-blow as merciful release), or as authentic dissent (her position registers a legitimate response to the apparent injustice). The chapter does not editorialize; Job's response in v10 sets the verdict the chapter wants — but it does not condemn the wife as such.

10
He said to her, "You are speaking as one of the foolish women speaks. Should we accept good from the Elohim and not accept bad?" In all this Job did not sin with his lips.
But he said unto her, Thou speakest as one of the foolish women speaketh. What? shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil? In all this did not Job sin with his lips.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֵלֶ֗יהָ כְּדַבֵּ֞ר אַחַ֤ת הַנְּבָלוֹת֙ תְּדַבֵּ֔רִי גַּ֣ם אֶת־הַטּ֗וֹב נְקַבֵּל֙ מֵאֵ֣ת הָאֱלֹהִ֔ים וְאֶת־הָרָ֖ע לֹ֣א נְקַבֵּ֑ל בְּכָל־זֹ֛את לֹא־חָטָ֥א אִיּ֖וֹב בִּשְׂפָתָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Job's second response. Gam et ha-tov n'qabel me-et ha-Elohim v'et ha-ra lo n'qabel — should we accept the good from the Elohim and not accept the bad? The question is rhetorical: the symmetry of acceptance is required by the relationship. If you accept the good as coming from a single source, you accept the bad as coming from the same source. // The verbal pair tov v-ra (good and bad) is the same vocabulary as Gen 2:9, 2:17 (the tree of da'at tov v-ra — knowing good and bad) and Gen 3:5, 3:22 (the human became like the Elohim knowing good and bad). The chapter is invoking the Edenic-vocabulary cluster: humans know good and bad. The capacity to know good and bad (acquired in Gen 3) is here applied — Job knows both, and accepts both. // The verb n'qabel (we shall accept, from qibel — receive, accept) is the chapter's standing-claim verb. Job is not endorsing the bad; he is accepting it as coming from the same source as the good. The chapter is making a sophisticated theological-philosophical move: the categorial unity of good and bad in the divine economy. // B'khol-zot lo chata Iyov bisefatav — In all this Job did not sin with his lips. The narrator's verdict closes the second test, slightly modified from 1:22 (which closed lo chata Iyov v'lo natan tiflah l'Elohim — Job did not sin nor charged Elohim with anything unseemly). The 2:10 verdict adds bisefatav — with his lips — a slight narrowing that has been read as the chapter leaving room for what Job will eventually do with his thoughts in the later dialogues. The narrowing is subtle but textually present.

11
When the three companions of Job heard of all this evil that had come upon him, each of them came from his place: Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite — and they made an appointment together to come to console him and to comfort him.
Now when Job`s three friends heard of all this evil that was come upon him, they came every one from his own place: Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite, and they made an appointment together to come to bemoan him and to comfort him.
וַֽיִּשְׁמְע֞וּ שְׁלֹ֣שֶׁת ׀ רֵעֵ֣י אִיּ֗וֹב אֵ֣ת כָּל־הָרָעָ֣ה הַזֹּאת֮ הַבָּ֣אָה עָלָיו֒ וַיָּבֹ֙אוּ֙ אִ֣ישׁ מִמְּקֹמ֔וֹ אֱלִיפַ֤ז הַתֵּימָנִי֙ וּבִלְדַּ֣ד הַשּׁוּחִ֔י וְצוֹפַ֖ר הַנַּעֲמָתִ֑י וַיִּוָּעֲד֣וּ יַחְדָּ֔ו לָב֥וֹא לָנֽוּד־ל֖וֹ וּלְנַחֲמֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The three friends arrive. Elifaz ha-Temani — Eliphaz the Temanite — Teman is a region in Edom (the southern Transjordan), associated with wisdom-tradition (Jer 49:7). Bildad ha-Shuchi — Bildad the Shuhite — Shuah is one of Abraham's sons by Keturah (Gen 25:2), associated with the Aramean periphery. Tzofar ha-Na'amati — Zophar the Naamathite — Naamah is uncertain; possibly an Edomite-Aramean location. // All three are non-Israelite peripheral-Aramean Wisdom figures. The chapter is staging the dialogue that follows as Wisdom-tradition discourse among Aramean periphery sages — the same cultural register as Job himself (Job of Uz, also non-Israelite). The book is positioning itself in the international Wisdom-literature tradition of the ANE (Sumerian / Akkadian / Egyptian / Edomite Wisdom traditions; cognate with the Babylonian Theodicy and the Dialogue of Pessimism).

12
When they raised their eyes from afar, they did not recognize him. They raised their voices and wept; each tore his robe and threw dust upon his head, skyward.
And when they lifted up their eyes afar off, and knew him not, they lifted up their voice, and wept; and they rent every one his robe, and sprinkled dust upon their heads toward heaven.
וַיִּשְׂא֨וּ אֶת־עֵינֵיהֶ֤ם מֵרָחוֹק֙ וְלֹ֣א הִכִּירֻ֔הוּ וַיִּשְׂא֥וּ קוֹלָ֖ם וַיִּבְכּ֑וּ וַיִּקְרְעוּ֙ אִ֣ישׁ מְעִל֔וֹ וַיִּזְרְק֥וּ עָפָ֛ר עַל־רָאשֵׁיהֶ֖ם הַשָּׁמָֽיְמָה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

Three mourning rites. The friends do not recognize Job at first (the affliction has disfigured him beyond recognition). They then perform three mourning rites: yisu qolam va-yivku (raised their voices and wept), yiqra'u ish me'ilo (each tore his robe — the same rite Job performed at 1:20), yizr'qu afar al-rashehem ha-shamaymah (threw dust upon their heads, skyward). // The dust-throwing-skyward is iconographic Hebrew Bible mourning-imagery (cf. Lam 2:10, Josh 7:6, 1 Sam 4:12, Neh 9:1). The verbal direction ha-shamaymah (skyward) is unusual — the friends throw the dust upward, where it falls back down on them. The action is comprehensive: they place themselves in the same mourning-posture Job is already in.

13
They sat with him on the ground for seven days and seven nights, and none spoke a word to him, for they saw that his pain was very great.
So they sat down with him upon the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spake a word unto him: for they saw that his grief was very great.
וַיֵּשְׁב֤וּ אִתּוֹ֙ לָאָ֔רֶץ שִׁבְעַ֥ת יָמִ֖ים וְשִׁבְעַ֣ת לֵיל֑וֹת וְאֵין־דֹּבֵ֤ר אֵלָיו֙ דָּבָ֔ר כִּ֣י רָא֔וּ כִּֽי־גָדַ֥ל הַכְּאֵ֖ב מְאֹֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

Seven days of silent presence. Shivat yamim v'shivat leilot — seven days and seven nights. The number seven is the Hebrew Bible's mourning-week canonical duration (cf. Gen 50:10 of Joseph mourning for Jacob, 1 Sam 31:13 / 1 Chr 10:12 of mourning for Saul, Sir 22:12 of seven-day mourning for the dead). The chapter is staging the friends as observing the full mourning-week before any conversation begins. // Ein dover elav davar — none spoke a word to him. The silence is total. The chapter ends on this seven-day silent presence — the dialogue that begins in chapter 3 will open with Job himself speaking first (va-yiftach Iyov et pihu va-y'qalel et yomo — Job opened his mouth and cursed his day, 3:1). The narrative arc closes here: the prologue's wager has been laid (chapters 1-2), the test has been delivered, Job has held firm, the friends have arrived in solidarity, the silence has begun. // The dialogue-prologue is complete. The dialogue-proper (chapters 3-31), the Elihu speeches (32-37), and the divine speeches (38-42) will follow. This is the book's narrative-framing prologue.

Chapter 38

Job 38 — YHWH from the whirlwind: the cosmological challenge

1
Then YHWH answered Job from the whirlwind, and said:
After this opened Job his mouth, and cursed his day.
וַיַּֽעַן־יְהוָ֣ה אֶת־אִ֭יּוֹב מנ הסערה [מִ֥ן ׀] [הַסְּעָרָ֗ה] וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The voice comes from the whirlwind. Min ha-se'arah — from the whirlwind/storm. The noun se'arah (סְעָרָה) is the same word that opened Ezekiel's vision at Ezek 1:4 — ruach se'arah ba'ah min ha-tzafon — a stormy wind coming from the north. The chapter is staging YHWH's voice in the same meteorological-vehicular register as Ezekiel's chayyot-throne apparatus. // The verb vayya'an — answered — frames the speech as a response, not an unprompted address. The 35 chapters of preceding dialogue (Job's complaints, the friends' arguments, Elihu's interventions) constitute the question; YHWH's answer arrives in the storm. // Ketiv/qere note: the text has mn ha-se'arah as ketiv with the Masoretic vocalization breaking it into min + ha-se'arah as qere. The qere reading is straightforward; the ketiv may reflect a less-standard spelling. The translation uses the qere.

2
Who is this who darkens counsel with words without knowledge?
And Job answered and said:
מִ֤י זֶ֨ה ׀ מַחְשִׁ֖יךְ עֵצָ֥ה בְמִלִּ֗ין בְּֽלִי־דָֽעַת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The opening rebuke. Machshikh etzah — darkening counsel. The verb chashakh (darken, obscure) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for the bringing-of-darkness — same root as choshekh (darkness) of Gen 1:2. Etzah is counsel, plan, deliberative wisdom. // The challenge is rhetorical: who is the person obscuring counsel (the divine deliberative plan) with words without knowledge? The implied answer: Job — and by extension all his speakers. The 35 chapters of dialogue have been darkening the divine plan with insufficient information.

3
Gird up your loins like a strong man — I will question you, and you instruct me.
Let the day perish wherein I was born, And the night which said, There is a man-child conceived.
אֱזָר־נָ֣א כְגֶ֣בֶר חֲלָצֶ֑יךָ וְ֝אֶשְׁאָלְךָ֗ וְהוֹדִיעֵֽנִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The challenge-form. Ezar-na khe-gever chalatzekha — gird up your loins like a strong man. The verb azar (gird, belt up) names the act of preparing for combat or physical labor; gever is strong man, warrior (not just adam — human — but the warrior-specific noun). // The verb-sequence (esha'alekha v'hodi'eni — I will ask you and you instruct me) inverts the dialogue's prior direction. Job has been demanding YHWH's reply for 35 chapters; YHWH now reframes: you take the questioning position; I will inform you. The chapter is staging the reversal of the dialogue's address-direction.

4
Where were you when I founded the earth? Tell — if you know understanding.
Let that day be darkness; Let not God from above seek for it, Neither let the light shine upon it.
אֵיפֹ֣ה הָ֭יִיתָ בְּיָסְדִי־אָ֑רֶץ הַ֝גֵּ֗ד אִם־יָדַ֥עְתָּ בִינָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The cosmological-temporal challenge. Eyfo hayita b'yosdi-aretz — where were you when I founded the earth. The verb yasad (found, lay the foundation of) is the architectural-construction term; the chapter is invoking the canonical Hebrew Bible image of the earth as a built-structure (cf. Ps 24:2, Ps 102:25, Isa 48:13). // The challenge sets the chapter's structural premise: Job (and humans generally) were not present at the cosmological founding. The chapter will catalog the things-Job-was-not-present-for as the substance of YHWH's answer. The 'where were you' question is the chapter's keystone interrogative — it will recur in implicit form across the catalog.

5
Who set its measurements — for you must know — or who stretched the measuring line over it?
Let darkness and the shadow of death claim it for their own; Let a cloud dwell upon it; Let all that maketh black the day terrify it.
מִי־שָׂ֣ם מְ֭מַדֶּיהָ כִּ֣י תֵדָ֑ע א֤וֹ מִֽי־נָטָ֖ה עָלֶ֣יהָ קָּֽו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Architectural-measurement vocabulary. Memaddeyha — its measurements / dimensions. Qav — measuring line, plumb line. The chapter is invoking the toolkit of an architect / builder: someone who measures the dimensions and stretches the line over a site before construction. The chapter is staging YHWH-as-cosmic-architect, in technically-specified construction vocabulary.

6
On what were its bases sunk, or who laid its cornerstone —
As for that night, let thick darkness seize upon it: Let it not rejoice among the days of the year; Let it not come into the number of the months.
עַל־מָ֭ה אֲדָנֶ֣יהָ הָטְבָּ֑עוּ א֥וֹ מִֽי־יָ֝רָ֗ה אֶ֣בֶן פִּנָּתָֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Foundation-imagery continues. Adaneha (its bases) — the same word used for the bases of the Tabernacle pillars (Ex 26:19, etc.) — pedestals sunk into the ground. Even pinnatah — its cornerstone — the architectural-symbolic foundation-stone. // The chapter is naming the earth's foundation as a sunk-pedestal-and-cornerstone construction. The reading is technical-architectural, not metaphorical-vague. Whether this is meant as cosmological-mythopoetic (the earth resting on cosmic pillars in ANE cosmology) or as procedural-construction language (the earth founded as if on architectural pedestals) is part of the chapter's ambiguity. The translation preserves both possibilities.

7
when the morning stars sang together and all the sons of the Elohim shouted for joy?
Lo, let that night be barren; Let no joyful voice come therein.
בְּרָן־יַ֭חַד כּ֣וֹכְבֵי בֹ֑קֶר וַ֝יָּרִ֗יעוּ כָּל־בְּנֵ֥י אֱלֹהִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The third Hebrew Bible bnei-elohim attestation. Kol bnei Elohim va-yariyu — all the sons of the Elohim shouted for joy. The chapter places the bnei elohim — the divine-council class introduced in Gen 6:2-4 (the bnei ha-elohim who took daughters of the human as wives) and re-deployed in Job 1:6, 2:1 (the bnei ha-elohim presenting themselves before YHWH) — at the moment of cosmic founding. This is the third Hebrew Bible explicit attestation of the class. // Kokhvei voqer — morning stars — are placed in parallel with the bnei elohim. The two phrases form a poetic couplet, and the parallel reading identifies them: the morning stars sang / the bnei elohim shouted. The same beings are named in two registers — astronomical (stars) and divine-council (bnei elohim). // The lens reads this parallel with attention: the chapter is identifying the bnei elohim with celestial bodies / celestial-beings at the founding of the earth. The same identification recurs in later Hebrew Bible literature (Isa 14:13 'the stars of El', Dan 8:10 'the host of the heavens'); the LXX of Deut 32:8 reads bnei elohim where the Masoretic Text has bnei Yisrael — at minimum, the text-historical tradition preserves the bnei elohim-as-celestial-class reading. // The verb va-yariyu (hifil of rua — shout, raise a war-cry, sound an alarm) is military-celebratory. The bnei elohim are shouting at the cosmic founding — an organized choral acclamation.

8
Or who shut up the sea with doors when it burst forth, came out of the womb?
Let them curse it that curse the day, Who are ready to rouse up leviathan.
וַיָּ֣סֶךְ בִּדְלָתַ֣יִם יָ֑ם בְּ֝גִיח֗וֹ מֵרֶ֥חֶם יֵצֵֽא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Sea-cosmogony. The chapter is reusing the Hebrew Bible / ANE cosmogonic image of the sea-restraint: a primordial chaos-sea that has to be shut behind doors to contain it. The image matches the Mesopotamian Tiamat-restraint tradition and Genesis 1:9-10's gathering the waters / creating the dry land. // B'gicho me-rechem yetze — when it burst forth, came out of the womb. The chapter is using birth-imagery: the sea is born from a womb. The image personalizes the cosmic-restraint procedure: the sea is not just a mass of water but a creaturely-emerging entity that had to be delivered and then contained. // The lens reads the chapter's combined-architectural-and-birth imagery: the cosmos is being built (vv 4-6) and the chaos is being birthed-and-contained (vv 8-11). Both vocabularies operate together.

9
when I made the cloud its garment, and thick darkness its swaddling band,
Let the stars of the twilight thereof be dark: Let it look for light, but have none; Neither let it behold the eyelids of the morning:
בְּשׂוּמִ֣י עָנָ֣ן לְבֻשׁ֑וֹ וַ֝עֲרָפֶ֗ל חֲתֻלָּתֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The sea's birth-clothing. Anan l'vusho — the cloud as its garment. Arafel chatulato — thick darkness as its swaddling band (chatulah — swaddling cloth — is a hapax, but transparent from chatal, a verb related to wrapping). // The chapter continues the birth-imagery: as a newborn is swaddled, the new-born sea is swaddled in cloud and darkness. The image is striking — the chaos-waters are wrapped, clothed, contained in the same way an infant is wrapped at birth.

10
and I broke my boundary over it and set a bar and doors;
Because it shut not up the doors of my [mother`s] womb, Nor hid trouble from mine eyes.
וָאֶשְׁבֹּ֣ר עָלָ֣יו חֻקִּ֑י וָֽ֝אָשִׂ֗ים בְּרִ֣יחַ וּדְלָתָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Eshbor alav chuqqi — I broke my boundary over it. The verb shavar (break) is unusual here; the standard cognate-construction would be sim chok (set a boundary). The chapter uses broke my boundary on it — possibly a hapax-style construction meaning imposed the boundary by breaking it through the unbounded sea. // Beriach u-dlatayim — a bar and doors — names the gate-and-bolt of containment. The image: the sea is behind a barred gate.

11
and I said, "This far you shall come and no further, and here shall the pride of your waves be stayed."
Why died I not from the womb? Why did I not give up the ghost when my mother bare me?
וָאֹמַ֗ר עַד־פֹּ֣ה תָ֭בוֹא וְלֹ֣א תֹסִ֑יף וּפֹ֥א־יָ֝שִׁ֗ית בִּגְא֥וֹן גַּלֶּֽיךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Ad-po tavo v-lo tosif — this far you shall come and no further. The chapter completes the sea-restraint with a direct quotation of YHWH's commanding word. The phrase is one of the Hebrew Bible's most-quoted boundary-setting formulas. // G'on galekha — the pride of your waves. Ga'on (pride, exaltation, majesty) is the chapter's personalizing register: the waves have pride. The chaos-sea is treated as a creaturely entity capable of pridefulness that has to be put-in-its-place by command.

12
Have you ever in your days commanded the morning, or caused the dawn to know its place,
Why did the knees receive me? Or why the breast, that I should suck?
הְֽ֭מִיָּמֶיךָ צִוִּ֣יתָ בֹּ֑קֶר ידעתה שחר [יִדַּ֖עְתָּה] [הַשַּׁ֣חַר] מְקֹמֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The challenge shifts to daily-cyclic phenomena. The chapter pivots from cosmogonic foundation-events to recurring atmospheric phenomena. The morning, the dawn, the day-night cycle. The interrogative ha-mi-yamekha — have you ever in your days — frames the question in terms of recurring cosmic management, not just origins. // Ketiv/qere note: yedaeta ha-shachar (qere) for yidatah shachar (ketiv). The qere is the standard reading: 'have you caused the dawn to know'.

13
to take hold of the wings of the land, that the wicked may be shaken out of it?
For now should I have lain down and been quiet; I should have slept; then had I been at rest,
לֶ֭אֱחֹז בְּכַנְפ֣וֹת הָאָ֑רֶץ וְיִנָּעֲר֖וּ רְשָׁעִ֣ים מִמֶּֽנָּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The morning as a corrective-mechanism. Le'echoz b'kanfot ha-aretz — to take hold of the wings of the land. The image: dawn grabs the corners of the earth (literally wings — same word as in bird-wings or garment-corners) and shakes out the wicked. The wicked who hid under cover of night are exposed at daybreak. The chapter is staging the day-night cycle as a judicial mechanism — the daily-dawn function is to expose wrongdoing.

14
It is changed like clay under a seal, and they stand out like a garment.
With kings and counsellors of the earth, Who built up waste places for themselves;
תִּ֭תְהַפֵּךְ כְּחֹ֣מֶר חוֹתָ֑ם וְ֝יִֽתְיַצְּב֗וּ כְּמ֣וֹ לְבֽוּשׁ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Two technological images. K'chomer chotam — like clay under a seal. The image is the cylinder-seal pressed into clay (Mesopotamian sealing-practice) — the earth as a clay surface that receives the morning's imprint, producing a pattern. Yityatzvu k'mo l'vush — they stand out like a garment — the night's hidden features become visible as morning dresses the land. // Both images are technical-craft (seal-making, weaving). The chapter is staging the daily-dawn as a craft-procedural event.

15
Their light is withheld from the wicked, and the uplifted arm is broken.
Or with princes that had gold, Who filled their houses with silver:
וְיִמָּנַ֣ע מֵרְשָׁעִ֣ים אוֹרָ֑ם וּזְר֥וֹעַ רָ֝מָ֗ה תִּשָּׁבֵֽר׃
16
Have you come to the springs of the sea, or walked in the searching of the deep?
Or as a hidden untimely birth I had not been, As infants that never saw light.
הֲ֭בָאתָ עַד־נִבְכֵי־יָ֑ם וּבְחֵ֥קֶר תְּ֝ה֗וֹם הִתְהַלָּֽכְתָּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The deep-investigation challenge. Nivkhei yam — springs of the sea — nevekh (נֵבֶךְ) is a hapax, but probably means deep-source, abyssal-spring. The chapter is naming the deep submarine source-points of the sea, not the surface. Cheker tehom — the searching/probing of the deep. Cheker (חֵקֶר) is investigation, search — a deliberate exploratory verb. // The question is whether Job has been to the deep submarine sources — has he done the deep-sea exploration? Tehom is the same word as in Gen 1:2 — the primordial deep, etymologically cognate with Akkadian tiāmtu/Tiamat (cf. glossary entry tehom). // The lens reads this with interest: the chapter is staging the deep submarine domain as a place that requires investigation — that has springs (specific source-points) and that allows walking-about in its searching. The image is exploratory-investigative.

17
Have the gates of death been revealed to you, or have you seen the gates of deep darkness?
There the wicked cease from troubling; And there the weary are at rest.
הֲנִגְל֣וּ לְ֭ךָ שַׁעֲרֵי־מָ֑וֶת וְשַׁעֲרֵ֖י צַלְמָ֣וֶת תִּרְאֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Death-domain challenge. Sha'arei mavet v-sha'arei tzalmavet — gates of death and gates of deep darkness. // Sha'arei mavet recurs in Hebrew Bible at Ps 9:14, 107:18 — gates of the realm-of-the-dead. Tzalmavet (צַלְמָוֶת) is variously analyzed: shadow of death (the traditional reading, tzel + mavet) or deep darkness (modern reading, from a root tzalam meaning 'be very dark'). The combined image: there are gates into the death-domain, with their own architecture and access-controls. // The chapter is staging the death-domain as a bounded territory with entry-gates — not as a generic dissolution. The reading-question Job is being asked: have you accessed these gates? Have you seen them?

18
Have you considered the breadth of the land? Tell — if you know all of it.
There the prisoners are at ease together; They hear not the voice of the taskmaster.
הִ֭תְבֹּנַנְתָּ עַד־רַחֲבֵי־אָ֑רֶץ הַ֝גֵּ֗ד אִם־יָדַ֥עְתָּ כֻלָּֽהּ׃
19
Where is the way to where the light dwells, and darkness — where is its place?
The small and the great are there: And the servant is free from his master.
אֵי־זֶ֣ה הַ֭דֶּרֶךְ יִשְׁכָּן־א֑וֹר וְ֝חֹ֗שֶׁךְ אֵי־זֶ֥ה מְקֹמֽוֹ׃
20
that you might take it to its boundary, that you might understand the paths to its house?
Wherefore is light given to him that is in misery, And life unto the bitter in soul;
כִּ֣י תִ֭קָּחֶנּוּ אֶל־גְּבוּל֑וֹ וְכִֽי־תָ֝בִ֗ין נְתִיב֥וֹת בֵּיתֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Light and darkness as locations. Vv 19-20 stage light and darkness as places-with-dwellings: each has a way, a house, paths, boundaries. The chapter is treating the light-dark cycle as a commuting phenomenon — light dwells somewhere and travels from there; darkness has its own residence. // The poetic-cosmological image — light dwells, darkness has its place — matches the Hebrew Bible's broader cosmology where day and night are quasi-personalized entities with locations (cf. Ps 19:5, where the sun has a tent it leaves from each morning). // The challenge: does Job know the paths to the house of light? The answer is plainly no — the chapter is exposing the limit of human-positional knowledge.

21
You know — for then you were born, and the number of your days is great.
Who long for death, but it cometh not, And dig for it more than for hid treasures;
יָ֭דַעְתָּ כִּי־אָ֣ז תִּוָּלֵ֑ד וּמִסְפַּ֖ר יָמֶ֣יךָ רַבִּֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

Ironic concession. The verse is read sarcastically: you know, of course, because you were born back then (i.e., at the cosmic founding) and your days are many (i.e., as long as the cosmos). The chapter is mocking the implicit-presumption of Job's prior speeches: the human-temporal scale is being thrown into relief against the cosmic-temporal scale.

22
Have you entered the storehouses of snow, or seen the storehouses of hail —
Who rejoice exceedingly, And are glad, when they can find the grave?
הֲ֭בָאתָ אֶל־אֹצְר֣וֹת שָׁ֑לֶג וְאֹצְר֖וֹת בָּרָ֣ד תִּרְאֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The weather-storehouses. Otzrot sheleg v-otzrot barad — storehouses of snow and storehouses of hail. The Hebrew noun otzar (אוֹצָר) is the standard term for a storehouse, treasury, armory — Joseph's grain-storehouses (Gen 41:56), the Temple-treasures (1 Kgs 7:51), military arsenals (Jer 50:25). // The image is striking: weather phenomena (snow, hail) are stored in storehouses — physical containers that hold them until they are released. The next verse will name the release-purpose: trouble, war.

23
which I have reserved for the time of distress, for the day of battle and war?
[Why is light given] to a man whose way is hid, And whom God hath hedged in?
אֲשֶׁר־חָשַׂ֥כְתִּי לְעֶת־צָ֑ר לְי֥וֹם קְ֝רָ֗ב וּמִלְחָמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The storehouses as armory. Chasakhti l-et tzar l-yom q'rav u-milchamah — I have reserved for the time of distress, for the day of battle and war. The chapter is naming the weather-storehouses as military reserves — snow and hail are strategic weapons deployed at specified times. // The reading is consistent with broader Hebrew Bible weather-as-divine-weapon imagery: hail in Josh 10:11 (the Amorite-coalition defeat), Ex 9:18-26 (the seventh Egyptian plague), Ezek 13:11-13, 38:22 (eschatological judgment hailstorms). The chapter is staging the weather-system as a deployable arsenal.

24
Where is the way the light is divided, the east wind scattered upon the earth?
For my sighing cometh before I eat, And my groanings are poured out like water.
אֵי־זֶ֣ה הַ֭דֶּרֶךְ יֵחָ֣לֶק א֑וֹר יָפֵ֖ץ קָדִ֣ים עֲלֵי־אָֽרֶץ׃
25
Who cleft a channel for the flood, and a way for the thunderstorm,
For the thing which I fear cometh upon me, And that which I am afraid of cometh unto me.
מִֽי־פִלַּ֣ג לַשֶּׁ֣טֶף תְּעָלָ֑ה וְ֝דֶ֗רֶךְ לַחֲזִ֥יז קֹלֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Atmospheric-channel imagery. T'alah la-sheteph — a channel for the flood. Derekh la-chaziz qolot — a way for the thunderstorm. The chapter is staging atmospheric phenomena as routed via designed channels: flood-water has its specified channel; thunderstorms have their specified pathways. The image is canal-system / waterworks engineering applied to the atmosphere.

26
to cause rain to fall on the land — no one there — the desert with no human in it,
I am not at ease, neither am I quiet, neither have I rest; But trouble cometh.
לְ֭הַמְטִיר עַל־אֶ֣רֶץ לֹא־אִ֑ישׁ מִ֝דְבָּ֗ר לֹא־אָדָ֥ם בּֽוֹ׃
27
to saturate desolate wasteland and cause the growth of vegetation to sprout?
לְהַשְׂבִּ֣יעַ שֹׁ֭אָה וּמְשֹׁאָ֑ה וּ֝לְהַצְמִ֗יחַ מֹ֣צָא דֶֽשֶׁא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The chapter's quiet aside. Rain falls on the desert where no human lives. The chapter is noting that the divine weather-system operates independently of human benefit — rain goes where no human is, watering desolate wasteland and bringing forth vegetation that no human will harvest. The cosmic-management runs on its own terms, not on terms of human-utility.

28
Does the rain have a father, or who fathered the drops of dew?
הֲיֵשׁ־לַמָּטָ֥ר אָ֑ב א֥וֹ מִי־ה֝וֹלִ֗יד אֶגְלֵי־טָֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

Birth-imagery for atmospheric phenomena. The chapter continues the cosmic birth register from vv 8-9: now rain and dew are fathered / birthed. The image is generative-genealogical: weather phenomena have parents, originators.

29
From whose womb came the ice, and the frost of the skies — who bore it?
מִבֶּ֣טֶן מִ֭י יָצָ֣א הַקָּ֑רַח וּכְפֹ֥ר שָׁ֝מַיִם מִ֣י יְלָדֽוֹ׃
30
The waters become hidden like stone, and the face of the deep is captured.
כָּ֭אֶבֶן מַ֣יִם יִתְחַבָּ֑אוּ וּפְנֵ֥י תְ֝ה֗וֹם יִתְלַכָּֽדוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 30

Freezing imagery. Ka-even mayim yitchabba'u — the waters become hidden like stone. P'nei tehom yitlakkadu — the face of the deep is captured/seized. The chapter is describing the freezing of water-surfaces in technical language: water becoming stone-like (ice), the deep being captured / gripped (frozen). // Verbs are precise: chava (be hidden, withdraw) names the transformation of liquid to solid as a hiding — the liquid waters hidden inside their own frozen surface. Lakhad (capture, seize) names the freezing of the deep as a capture-event.

31
Can you bind the chains of Kimah, or loose the cords of Kesil?
הַֽ֭תְקַשֵּׁר מַעֲדַנּ֣וֹת כִּימָ֑ה אֽוֹ־מֹשְׁכ֖וֹת כְּסִ֣יל תְּפַתֵּֽחַ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 31

The constellations enter. Kimah (כִּימָה) is generally identified with the Pleiades (the seven sister-stars in Taurus); Kesil (כְּסִיל) with Orion (the canonical hunter-constellation). The same pair appears in Job 9:9 and Amos 5:8. // The image-language is specific: ma'adannot Kimah — the chains of Kimah (Pleiades bound in cluster-formation); mosh'khot Kesil — the cords of Kesil (Orion held by his belt-and-bands). The chapter is reading the constellations as physically-restrained patterns — bound into their formations by cosmic ropes / chains. The challenge: can Job bind or loose these cosmic-tied configurations? // The lens reads this verse with strong interest. The Wheel of Heaven framework engages the precession-of-the-equinoxes and the cosmic-clock astronomy as a load-bearing element of the project's cosmology. Job 38:31 is one of the Hebrew Bible's most explicit named-constellation references — the chapter has the canonical Hebrew Bible vocabulary for the Pleiades-Orion cluster, which the precession-of-the-equinoxes calculations have used as reference-stars for millennia. The chapter's stated challenge (can you bind/loose these?) acknowledges the constellations as fixed-cycle astronomical structures whose timing and configuration are not subject to human manipulation.

32
Can you bring forth the Mazzaroth in its appointed time, or guide Ayish with her children?
הֲתֹצִ֣יא מַזָּר֣וֹת בְּעִתּ֑וֹ וְ֝עַ֗יִשׁ עַל־בָּנֶ֥יהָ תַנְחֵֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 32

Two more astronomical-class names. Mazzarot (מַזָּרוֹת) is a hapax (occurring only here). The traditional identification (LXX, Vulgate) is the zodiac constellations — the twelve constellations of the ecliptic through which the sun, moon, and planets appear to move. The word may be cognate with Akkadian manzaltu (a celestial-position term) or with the Hebrew root zarah (scatter — possibly the scattered band of zodiacal constellations). The Mishnaic Hebrew mazzal (mazzaltot in plural — fortune-stars) descends from this term. // Be-ito — in its appointed time — names the timed cycle: the zodiac is brought-forth on schedule, in the precise calendrical rhythm. // Ayish (עַיִשׁ) — the Bear constellation — is generally identified with Ursa Major (the Great Bear), also called Ash (עָשׁ) at Job 9:9. Bnei Ayish — the children of Ayish — names the cluster of stars forming the Bear-constellation. // The four named celestial objects of vv 31-32 (Kimah/Pleiades, Kesil/Orion, Mazzarot/Zodiac, Ayish/Bear) are the canonical Hebrew Bible astronomical-reference catalogue. The chapter is staging Job's confrontation with timed-cyclical cosmic astronomy as something he plainly does not control. // For the WoH framework specifically: the Mazzarot timing-cycle is the chapter's most direct invocation of the calendrical-zodiacal astronomy that the precession-of-the-equinoxes framework operates within. The Hebrew text has, in this verse, the technical vocabulary for the cosmic-clock the Wheel of Heaven engages.

33
Do you know the ordinances of the skies? Can you set its dominion on the land?
הֲ֭יָדַעְתָּ חֻקּ֣וֹת שָׁמָ֑יִם אִם־תָּשִׂ֖ים מִשְׁטָר֣וֹ בָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 33

Chuqot shamayim — ordinances of the skies. The compound names fixed cosmic laws / regulations governing the heavens. Chuqot (חֻקּוֹת) is the plural of choq — statute, ordinance, prescribed rule. The chapter is asserting that the heavens operate under prescribed law-like regularity — chuqot, statutes, not random behavior. // The compound chuqot shamayim recurs in Jer 33:25 (YHWH's covenant with day and night, chuqot shamayim va-aretz — the ordinances of the heavens and the earth). The chapter's framing is consistent with the broader Hebrew Bible cosmological vocabulary: cosmic operations are lawful, predictable, calendrically-tracked. // Mishtaro ba-aretz — its dominion on the land. Mishtar (משׁטר) is a hapax-style noun from the root shatar (rule, govern). The image: the skies govern the earth via their ordinances. The astrological-influence model is implicit — what happens in the heavens rules what happens on the land. The chapter is engaging the broader ANE astrological-cosmological framework directly.

34
Can you lift up your voice to the cloud — that abundance of water cover you?
הֲתָרִ֣ים לָעָ֣ב קוֹלֶ֑ךָ וְֽשִׁפְעַת־מַ֥יִם תְּכַסֶּֽךָּ׃
35
Can you send out lightnings, that they go and say to you, "Here we are"?
הַֽתְשַׁלַּ֣ח בְּרָקִ֣ים וְיֵלֵ֑כוּ וְיֹאמְר֖וּ לְךָ֣ הִנֵּֽנוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 35

Lightnings respond to direct command. Vayoremu lekha hinnenu — and they say to you, 'here we are'. The personifying touch is striking: the lightnings speak, responding to dispatch with the standard Hebrew Bible report-formula hinneni / hinnenu (here I am / here we are) — the same formula Moses and Samuel and Isaiah use when YHWH calls them (Ex 3:4, 1 Sam 3:4-8, Isa 6:8). The chapter is staging lightning-bolts as responsive subordinates — sent on mission, reporting in.

36
Who placed wisdom in the inward parts, or who gave understanding to the mind?
מִי־שָׁ֭ת בַּטֻּח֣וֹת חָכְמָ֑ה א֤וֹ מִֽי־נָתַ֖ן לַשֶּׂ֣כְוִי בִינָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 36

A contested verse. Two hapax-style nouns: tuchot (טֻחוֹת — inward parts, hidden interior; possibly ibis — the Egyptian sacred bird associated with wisdom) and sekhvi (שֶׂכְוִי — rooster, in later Hebrew; here possibly mind, heart-as-locus-of-thought). The verse's exact subject is unresolved. // Three readings circulate: (1) hidden interior (the seat of human wisdom located in the inward organs); (2) animal-wisdom (the verse names two birds — ibis and rooster — as wisdom-instinct examples, consistent with the chapter's animal-catalog in vv 39-41); (3) clouds-and-meteors (taking tuchot and sekhvi as obscure atmospheric phenomena). The chapter does not specify. The translation goes with the inward-parts reading; commentary surfaces the cruces.

37
Who can count the clouds in wisdom, or tip the bottles of the skies,
מִֽי־יְסַפֵּ֣ר שְׁחָקִ֣ים בְּחָכְמָ֑ה וְנִבְלֵ֥י שָׁ֝מַ֗יִם מִ֣י יַשְׁכִּֽיב׃
38
when the dust pours into hardness and the clods cling together?
בְּצֶ֣קֶת עָ֭פָר לַמּוּצָ֑ק וּרְגָבִ֥ים יְדֻבָּֽקוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 38

A drought-and-rain image. Dust pours like molten metal (la-mutzaq — into the molten / poured-thing); clods cling together — the chapter is describing the moisture-cycle that transforms loose dust into clinging earth. The image is alchemical-procedural: dry dust becomes wet aggregate becomes solid earth through the rain-cycle.

39
Can you hunt prey for the lioness, or fill the appetite of the young lions,
הֲתָצ֣וּד לְלָבִ֣יא טָ֑רֶף וְחַיַּ֖ת כְּפִירִ֣ים תְּמַלֵּֽא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 39

The chapter pivots to the animal kingdom. The first divine speech (chs 38-39) divides structurally: vv 4-38 are cosmological-atmospheric; vv 39-41 introduce the animal-world catalog that will run through chapter 39. // Lavi (לָבִיא) — lioness — names the predatory cat-class; kfirim (כְּפִירִים) is young lions (lions in their prime). The chapter is asking whether Job can provide for the predators — feed the carnivorous food-chain. The implicit framing: the food-chain itself is divinely-managed.

40
when they crouch in their dens, sit in their lair to lie in ambush?
כִּי־יָשֹׁ֥חוּ בַמְּעוֹנ֑וֹת יֵשְׁב֖וּ בַסֻּכָּ֣ה לְמוֹ־אָֽרֶב׃
41
Who prepares prey for the raven when its young cry to El, wander without food?
מִ֤י יָכִ֥ין לָעֹרֵ֗ב צֵ֫יד֥וֹ כִּֽי־ילדו [יְ֭לָדָיו] אֶל־אֵ֣ל יְשַׁוֵּ֑עוּ יִ֝תְע֗וּ לִבְלִי־אֹֽכֶל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 41

The chapter closes on the raven. La-orev tzeydo — prey for the raven. The same bird as the one Noah sent out first from the ark in Gen 8:7. The raven is the canonical Hebrew Bible scavenger-bird, often appearing alongside ritually-unclean associations. The chapter is staging the raven's chick-feeding as a divinely-managed function. // Yeshawe'u — cry out — from shava (cry, call for help). The raven-chicks cry to El — a striking moment. The chapter is naming animal-prayer / animal-distress-calls as addressed to El (the broader-divine class-name, the same root as Elohim). The animal world prays; the chapter affirms that the prayers are heard. // Ketiv/qere note: yeladav (qere) for yldw (ketiv). The qere is the standard masoretic vocalization. // The chapter ends — chapter 39 will continue the divine speech with an extended catalog of animals (the wild donkey, wild ox, ostrich, horse, hawk, eagle).

Chapter 40

Job 40 — Interim response, second speech opener, Behemoth, and the start of Leviathan

1
YHWH answered Job and said:
Then answered Eliphaz the Temanite, and said,
וַיַּ֖עַן יְהוָ֥ה אֶת־אִיּ֗וֹב וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The first speech closes; the address-formula returns. The verse marks the structural boundary between YHWH's first speech (Job 38–39) and the brief interim before the second speech (40:6 onward). The same verbal form (vayya'an YHWH et-Iyov) opens at 38:1; here it closes the first speech with a brief return-of-address.

2
Shall the contender with Shaddai instruct him? Let the one who would rebuke Eloah answer it.
If one assay to commune with thee, wilt thou be grieved? But who can withhold himself from speaking?
הֲ֭רֹב עִם־שַׁדַּ֣י יִסּ֑וֹר מוֹכִ֖יחַ אֱל֣וֹהַּ יַעֲנֶֽנָּה׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Two divine-name registers in one verse. Shaddai (שַׁדַּי) — the patriarchal divine name (see qol-mayim-rabbim for the etymology). Eloah (אֱלוֹהַּ) — the rarer singular form of Elohim, appearing 52 times in the Hebrew Bible, 41 of which are in Job. The book's distinctive divine-name register is Eloah-Shaddai-El (the patriarchal-Aramean cluster) rather than YHWH-Elohim (the Israelite-covenantal cluster). The chapter is in international Wisdom-literature register. // The challenge is the chapter's signature reversal: the one who contends (rob, the same root as rib — lawsuit) with Shaddai is now invited to instruct him. Job is being given the floor — his 35 chapters of complaint are positioned as an implicit lawsuit, and now the case-record is offered to him.

3
Job answered YHWH and said:
Behold, thou hast instructed many, And thou hast strengthened the weak hands.
וַיַּ֖עַן אִיּ֥וֹב אֶת־יְהוָ֗ה וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
4
Behold, I am of small account — what shall I answer you? My hand I lay on my mouth.
Thy words have upholden him that was falling, And thou hast made firm the feeble knees.
הֵ֣ן קַ֭לֹּתִי מָ֣ה אֲשִׁיבֶ֑ךָּ יָ֝דִ֗י שַׂ֣מְתִּי לְמוֹ־פִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Job's first interim response. Qaloti — I am of small account (from qalal, be light, of little weight).
