The chapter has been weighing Job in the divine-cosmic register; Job acknowledges he comes out light. // Yadi samti l-mo-pi — my hand I lay on my mouth — the canonical gesture of imposed silence (cf. Judg 18:19, Prov 30:32, Mic 7:16). Job is silencing himself.

5
Once I have spoken, and I will not answer; twice — and I will not add.
But now it is come unto thee, and thou faintest; It toucheth thee, and thou art troubled.
אַחַ֣ת דִּ֭בַּרְתִּי וְלֹ֣א אֶֽעֱנֶ֑ה וּ֝שְׁתַּ֗יִם וְלֹ֣א אוֹסִֽיף׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The X-then-Y+1 climactic-number formula. Achat dibarti ... u-shtayim v-lo osif — once I have spoken ... twice and I will not add. The chapter uses the standard Hebrew Bible escalating-number formula (X-then-X+1): one then two, parallel to Prov 6:16 (six then seven), Prov 30:15-31 (three then four, repeatedly), Amos 1:3 onwards (three transgressions of X, then four). The formula compresses I have spoken many times and will speak no more into a tight numeric climax. // Job's response is properly humble but not yet complete — chapter 42 will give the final acknowledgment. This is the interim response: silence, withdrawal, deference. The full retraction comes later.

6
YHWH answered Job from the whirlwind and said:
Is not thy fear [of God] thy confidence, [And] the integrity of thy ways thy hope?
וַיַּֽעַן־יְהוָ֣ה אֶת־אִ֭יּוֹב מנ סערה [מִ֥ן ׀] [סְעָרָ֗ה] וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The second speech opens, identically to the first. Min se'arah — from the whirlwind — the same source-formula as 38:1. The chapter pivots from the interim response back to direct divine address. The verbal parallel (38:1 / 40:6) frames the two divine speeches as bracketed by the same storm-vehicle vocabulary. // Ketiv/qere note: min se'arah (ketiv, without the article) → min ha-se'arah (qere, with the article). The qere reading restores the article, matching 38:1's min ha-se'arah exactly.

7
Gird up your loins like a strong man — I will question you, and you instruct me.
Remember, I pray thee, who [ever] perished, being innocent? Or where were the upright cut off?
אֱזָר־נָ֣א כְגֶ֣בֶר חֲלָצֶ֑יךָ אֶ֝שְׁאָלְךָ֗ וְהוֹדִיעֵֽנִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The verse is identical to 38:3. The chapter is consciously bracketing the two divine speeches with the same opening challenge-formula. The reader is being told: the second speech will follow the same rhetorical pattern as the first — question and answer-required. But where the first speech catalogued the cosmos and creatures Job did not control, the second speech will name two specific creatures — Behemoth and Leviathan — that Job cannot subdue. The second speech is focused, not catalog-broad.

8
Would you actually annul my judgment? Would you condemn me so that you may be justified?
According as I have seen, they that plow iniquity, And sow trouble, reap the same.
הַ֭אַף תָּפֵ֣ר מִשְׁפָּטִ֑י תַּ֝רְשִׁיעֵ֗נִי לְמַ֣עַן תִּצְדָּֽק׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The chapter's structural challenge. Two interrogative verbs: tafer mishpati (annul my judgment) and tarshi'eni l-ma'an titzdaq (condemn me so that you may be justified). The chapter is naming the implicit position of Job's complaint: in arguing his innocence, Job has functionally placed YHWH in the wrong. The chapter is making the structural-juridical observation explicit.

9
Or do you have an arm like El? With a voice like his can you thunder?
By the breath of God they perish, And by the blast of his anger are they consumed.
וְאִם־זְר֖וֹעַ כָּאֵ֥ל ׀ לָ֑ךְ וּ֝בְק֗וֹל כָּמֹ֥הוּ תַרְעֵֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

Zeroa ka-El — an arm like El. The divine-name El (the broader-class, the same root as Elohim) is invoked. Zeroa — arm — is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for strength, military force, agency (cf. Ex 6:6 — YHWH's outstretched arm). // The chapter is asking: do you have divine-scale agency? The matching second clause asks: do you have divine-scale voice (the storm-voice that thunders)? Job is being measured against the divine power-class. The implicit answer: no. // The verse sets up the chapter's narrowing focus. The first speech (chs 38–39) catalogued the cosmos. The second speech now narrows to: do you have the physical power to humble what needs humbling? That power-test is the entry-condition for the Behemoth-and-Leviathan section that follows.

10
Adorn yourself with majesty and exaltation — be clothed in honor and splendor.
The roaring of the lion, and the voice of the fierce lion, And the teeth of the young lions, are broken.
עֲדֵ֥ה נָ֣א גָֽא֣וֹן וָגֹ֑בַהּ וְה֖וֹד וְהָדָ֣ר תִּלְבָּֽשׁ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The challenge to take the throne. Four nouns of divine regalia: ga'on (majesty), gobah (exaltation, height), hod (honor, glory), hadar (splendor). The chapter is challenging Job to put on — clothe yourself with — the divine regalia. The implicit framing: only the one wearing these can do what comes next.

11
Scatter the outbursts of your anger — look on everyone proud and humble him.
The old lion perisheth for lack of prey, And the whelps of the lioness are scattered abroad.
הָ֭פֵץ עֶבְר֣וֹת אַפֶּ֑ךָ וּרְאֵ֥ה כָל־גֵּ֝אֶ֗ה וְהַשְׁפִּילֵֽהוּ׃
12
Look on everyone proud and bring him low; tread down the wicked where they stand.
Now a thing was secretly brought to me, And mine ear received a whisper thereof.
רְאֵ֣ה כָל־גֵּ֭אֶה הַכְנִיעֵ֑הוּ וַהֲדֹ֖ךְ רְשָׁעִ֣ים תַּחְתָּֽם׃
13
Hide them in the dust together; bind up their faces in the hidden place.
In thoughts from the visions of the night, When deep sleep falleth on men,
טָמְנֵ֣ם בֶּעָפָ֣ר יָ֑חַד פְּ֝נֵיהֶ֗ם חֲבֹ֣שׁ בַּטָּמֽוּן׃
14
Then I myself would acknowledge you, for your own right hand has saved you.
Fear came upon me, and trembling, Which made all my bones to shake.
וְגַם־אֲנִ֥י אוֹדֶ֑ךָּ כִּֽי־תוֹשִׁ֖עַ לְךָ֣ יְמִינֶֽךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The conditional concession. V-gam ani odekka ki-toshia lekha y'minekha — Then I too would acknowledge you, that your own right hand has saved you. The chapter is staging a conditional: if Job can do the proud-humbling work (vv 11-13) by his own right hand, then YHWH would acknowledge his self-sufficient agency. The chapter is structurally setting up the physical-impossibility demonstration that follows. Verses 15-24 (Behemoth) and 25-32 + ch 41 (Leviathan) will name two specific creatures Job cannot even touch — let alone humble. The chapter's rhetorical move: how could you humble the proud-of-the-cosmos if you cannot even subdue a single named animal?

15
Look now at Behemoth, which I made along with you — he eats grass like the ox.
Then a spirit passed before my face; The hair of my flesh stood up.
הִנֵּה־נָ֣א בְ֭הֵמוֹת אֲשֶׁר־עָשִׂ֣יתִי עִמָּ֑ךְ חָ֝צִ֗יר כַּבָּקָ֥ר יֹאכֵֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

Behemoth is introduced. Behemoth (בְּהֵמוֹת) — the chapter's first named creature. The noun is grammatically the plural of behemah (beast, large quadruped) — a plural-of-majesty / plural-of-intensification form, naming a single super-class creature: the Beast par excellence. // Asher asiti immakh — which I made along with you. The phrase locates Behemoth in the same creation-context as the human — they are fellow-creatures. The chapter is positioning Behemoth as a real-creaturely entity, not a mythological abstraction. // Chatzir ka-baqar yokhel — he eats grass like the ox. Behemoth is a herbivorous mega-creature — the chapter opens its description by establishing the diet. // Identification. Modern scholarship has proposed three main identifications: (1) the hippopotamus (the standard scholarly default; the hippo's grass-eating, aquatic habitat, and massive size fit the description well); (2) a mythological-cosmological beast (the canonical land-counterpart to Leviathan-as-sea-beast, paralleling the Mesopotamian land/sea cosmic-creature pairs); (3) an extinct megafauna identification (some 19th-century readings proposed dinosaurs; this is the same creationist-tradition reading we engaged at Gen 1:21 with taninim, and it has the same critique-issues — the Hebrew word is not 'dinosaur' and the chapter does not specify). The translation preserves the Hebrew name Behemoth untranslated (matching Leviathan, the parallel sea-creature); the commentary surfaces the identification spectrum.

16
Behold now, his strength is in his loins, his power in the muscles of his belly.
It stood still, but I could not discern the appearance thereof; A form was before mine eyes: [There was] silence, and I heard a voice, [saying],
הִנֵּה־נָ֣א כֹח֣וֹ בְמָתְנָ֑יו וְ֝אֹנ֗וֹ בִּשְׁרִירֵ֥י בִטְנֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Anatomical strength-locus. Kocho be-motnav — his strength in his loins. Ono bi-sh'rirei bitno — his power in the muscles of his belly. The chapter is naming Behemoth's core musculature as the seat of his power. The combined loins + belly-muscles register matches large quadruped anatomy (heavy hindquarters + massive abdominal muscles for digestion of plant fiber and for support of mass).

17
He stiffens his tail like a cedar — the sinews of his thighs are knit together.
Shall mortal man be more just than God? Shall a man be more pure than his Maker?
יַחְפֹּ֣ץ זְנָב֣וֹ כְמוֹ־אָ֑רֶז גִּידֵ֖י פחדו [פַחֲדָ֣יו] יְשֹׂרָֽגוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The cedar-tail. Yachpotz znavo k'mo-erez — he stiffens his tail like a cedar. The phrase has been one of the chapter's interpretive flashpoints. // The Hebrew erez (cedar) is the Lebanon-cedar — a massive tree (heights up to 40m, trunk diameter up to 3m). The image: Behemoth's tail is stiff and massive like a cedar trunk. This is the description that makes the hippopotamus identification difficult — hippopotami do not have cedar-like tails (their tails are short and small relative to body mass). // The verse is often cited in the dinosaur-identification tradition (a sauropod tail matches 'cedar-like'). The lens reads the verse: the Hebrew describes a creature with a large stiff tail comparable to a tree-trunk. The hippopotamus identification requires reading zanav as a euphemism for a different body-part (some commentators have proposed the male reproductive organ — but this is contested and reads in something the Hebrew does not say). // Ketiv/qere: fachado (qere) for fchdv (ketiv) — his thighs (qere) for an unpointed consonantal text. The qere is the standard reading. Gidei fachadav — the sinews of his thighs — names the hind-leg connective tissue, knit together for support of mass.

18
His bones are channels of bronze, his limbs like bars of iron.
Behold, he putteth no trust in his servants; And his angels he chargeth with folly:
עֲ֭צָמָיו אֲפִיקֵ֣י נְחוּשָׁ֑ה גְּ֝רָמָ֗יו כִּמְטִ֥יל בַּרְזֶֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Bronze bones and iron bars. Atzamav afiqei nechushah — his bones are channels of bronze. Grama kim'til barzel — his limbs like bars of iron. // The metaphor is metallurgical-mechanical: Behemoth's skeleton is described in metallurgical-engineering register — bones as bronze pipes/channels (hollow, structural), limbs as iron bars (solid, rigid). The chapter is using the canonical Hebrew Bible metal-vocabulary (bronze = soft-strong; iron = harder-stiffer) to name the creature's structural composition. // The verse is the second of the chapter's hard-to-match-to-real-anatomy descriptions. Hippopotamus bones are not 'channels of bronze' in any natural reading. The chapter is using technical-engineering metaphor for a creature whose composition is being described as if it were a piece of metalwork.

19
He is the first of the ways of El — the one who made him brings his sword.
How much more them that dwell in houses of clay, Whose foundation is in the dust, Who are crushed before the moth!
ה֭וּא רֵאשִׁ֣ית דַּרְכֵי־אֵ֑ל הָ֝עֹשׂוֹ יַגֵּ֥שׁ חַרְבּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

Hu reshit darkhei El — he is the first of the ways of El. The chapter is naming Behemoth as the first-among-creation, the reshit (beginning, first-fruit) of the divine darkhei (ways, methods, projects). The same noun reshit opens Gen 1:1 (bereshit — in beginning). The chapter is using vocabulary that places Behemoth in the cosmogonic-first-creature position. // The same compound reshit darkhei appears in Prov 8:22 of personified Wisdom: YHWH qanani reshit darko — YHWH possessed me, the first of his ways. Both passages use reshit darkhei El/YHWH for a creature of singular cosmic-foundational status. // Ha-osho yaggesh charbo — the one who made him brings his sword. The interpretation is contested: possibly only his maker (i.e., YHWH) can wield a sword against him, or only his maker can approach him with weaponry. Either way: Behemoth is unsubdueable except by his maker.

20
For the mountains bring forth produce for him, and all the wild animals of the field play there.
Betwixt morning and evening they are destroyed: They perish for ever without any regarding it.
כִּֽי־ב֭וּל הָרִ֣ים יִשְׂאוּ־ל֑וֹ וְֽכָל־חַיַּ֥ת הַ֝שָּׂדֶ֗ה יְשַֽׂחֲקוּ־שָֽׁם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

The mountains feed him. Bul harim — the produce of the mountains (bul — a hapax-style noun, probably yield, produce, growth). The mountains yield up their produce to Behemoth. Even the mass-feeding-capacity has cosmic scope: Behemoth requires not local pasture but mountain-scale productivity. // V'khol chayyat ha-sadeh y'sachaqu sham — and all the wild animals of the field play there. The chapter pairs Behemoth's feeding-ground with the play-ground of the rest of the wild animals — the same place. The image: an open landscape where Behemoth grazes while smaller animals play in his vicinity. The combination paints Behemoth as vast but peaceable — not predatory toward the surrounding animals.

21
Under the lotus trees he lies, in the covert of reeds and marsh.
Is not their tent-cord plucked up within them? They die, and that without wisdom.
תַּֽחַת־צֶאֱלִ֥ים יִשְׁכָּ֑ב בְּסֵ֖תֶר קָנֶ֣ה וּבִצָּֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

Habitat. Tza'elim (צֶאֱלִים) — lotus trees — water-edge vegetation. Qaneh u-bitzah — reeds and marsh. The chapter places Behemoth in wetland habitat: lotus-covered, reed-fringed, marshy. The habitat-description is the strongest argument for the hippopotamus identification — hippos are semi-aquatic, spending their days submerged in marshes and emerging at night to graze. The wetland-habitat detail is consistent with hippo ecology in the Nile / Levantine marshes the Hebrew Bible's authors would have known about.

22
The lotus trees shade him in their shade; the willows of the brook surround him.
יְסֻכֻּ֣הוּ צֶאֱלִ֣ים צִֽלֲל֑וֹ יְ֝סֻבּ֗וּהוּ עַרְבֵי־נָֽחַל׃
23
Behold, the river surges — he is not alarmed; he is confident even if the Jordan rushes against his mouth.
הֵ֤ן יַעֲשֹׁ֣ק נָ֭הָר לֹ֣א יַחְפּ֑וֹז יִבְטַ֓ח ׀ כִּֽי־יָגִ֖יחַ יַרְדֵּ֣ן אֶל־פִּֽיהוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

Behemoth and the Jordan. Ya'ashoq nahar lo yachpoz — the river surges, he is not alarmed. Yivtach ki yagiach Yarden el-pihu — he is confident even if the Jordan rushes against his mouth. // The chapter names a specific river — the Jordan — as the test-case. The Jordan flows from north to south through the Levant, with major flow-surges in the spring. The chapter is staging Behemoth as un-disturbable by the Jordan's largest flow — he can drink the Jordan if it rushes into his mouth and not be alarmed. // The Jordan reference is geographically-specific. It places Behemoth's habitat in the Israelite-known river-system rather than an abstract cosmic-river — though the figure of speech may be hyperbolic (no actual hippo could swallow the Jordan). The reference at minimum locates the audience: Levantine, with the Jordan as the canonical Israelite reference-river.

24
Can one take him by his eyes? Can one pierce his nose with a snare?
בְּעֵינָ֥יו יִקָּחֶ֑נּוּ בְּ֝מֽוֹקְשִׁ֗ים יִנְקָב־אָֽף׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

Behemoth's capture-impossibility. The chapter closes the Behemoth section with two rhetorical questions about subduing Behemoth physically. Can one capture him through his eyes (i.e., by his attention) or by piercing his nose with a snare? The implicit answer: no. // The chapter is staging Behemoth as unsubdueable by human hunting-technology. The eye-and-nose hunting-vocabulary is precise: in ANE hunting-iconography, large animals are subdued by piercing the nose (nose-rings, harness-pierce-points) and by capture through visual-attraction (snare-traps positioned in lines of sight). Neither works on Behemoth. // The Behemoth section closes here. Verse 25 onwards (English 41:1-8) shifts to Leviathan — the parallel sea-creature.

25
Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook? Can you press down his tongue with a cord?
תִּמְשֹׁ֣ךְ לִוְיָתָ֣ן בְּחַכָּ֑ה וּ֝בְחֶ֗בֶל תַּשְׁקִ֥יעַ לְשֹׁנֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Leviathan enters. Livyatan (לִוְיָתָן) — the chapter's second named creature, paired with Behemoth as land-creature / sea-creature cosmic-pair. The name Livyatan is from a root lavah (twist, coil, wind) — the twisting one. The same creature is named at: Ps 74:14 (YHWH breaks the heads of Leviathan), Ps 104:26 (Leviathan plays in the sea), Isa 27:1 (YHWH will punish Livyatan nachash bariach v-Livyatan nachash aqallaton — Leviathan the fleeing serpent and Leviathan the twisting serpent — the tannin in the sea). // The Isaiah 27:1 verse is the critical cross-reference. It identifies Leviathan with the tannin — the same word the WoH translation renders as dragons in Gen 1:21 (see taninim glossary entry). Leviathan IS the tannin of the prophets; Leviathan IS the dragon-class creature. The chapter is now describing the canonical Hebrew Bible sea-dragon in extensive detail. // Ugaritic parallel. The Ugaritic Lotan (ltn) is a seven-headed sea-dragon defeated by Baal in the Baal Cycle. The Hebrew Livyatan is the same name with the addition of the suffix -yatan (possibly an adjectival or intensifier ending). The Hebrew Bible Leviathan is the same creature as the Ugaritic Lotan, demythologized but lexically preserved. // The chapter's first question is fishing-vocabulary: chakkah (fishhook), chevel (cord). Can Leviathan be caught with fishing equipment? The implicit answer: no.

26
Can you put a rope into his nose, or pierce his jaw with a hook?
הֲתָשִׂ֣ים אַגְמ֣וֹן בְּאַפּ֑וֹ וּ֝בְח֗וֹחַ תִּקּ֥וֹב לֶֽחֱיוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

More capture-equipment. Agmon (rope, reed, hook-line). Choach (thorn, hook). The chapter is asking: can you use the canonical fishing/livestock-control equipment on Leviathan? Implicit answer: no.

27
Will he multiply pleas to you? Will he speak soft words to you?
הֲיַרְבֶּ֣ה אֵ֭לֶיךָ תַּחֲנוּנִ֑ים אִם־יְדַבֵּ֖ר אֵלֶ֣יךָ רַכּֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

Leviathan as anthropomorphic-speaking creature. The verse implies Leviathan has the capacity for speech — would he plead with you, would he speak soft words? The implicit answer: no, he will not. The chapter is staging Leviathan as a being-with-agency, not just a creature-to-be-subdued; the rejection of supplication is part of his nature.

28
Will he cut a covenant with you, that you may take him as a slave forever?
הֲיִכְרֹ֣ת בְּרִ֣ית עִמָּ֑ךְ תִּ֝קָּחֶ֗נּוּ לְעֶ֣בֶד עוֹלָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

Hayikhrot brit immakh — will he cut a covenant with you? The chapter uses the technical covenant-vocabulary (karat brit — cut a covenant, the canonical Hebrew Bible covenant-establishing verb). // The verse is striking: Leviathan is a creature with the capacity to enter into covenants. The implication: Leviathan has the status to be a covenant-partner — not a beast, but an agent. The chapter has now classed Leviathan as a creature higher than the kind that could be reduced to eved olam (eternal slave). // The lens reads this with attention: Leviathan is described as having the political-relational capacity of a party. The chapter is staging Leviathan in a higher creaturely register than Behemoth (who was named as first of the ways of El, also high-status, but not as a covenant-capable agent).

29
Can you play with him as with a bird? Can you tie him up for your young girls?
הַֽתְשַׂחֶק־בּ֭וֹ כַּצִּפּ֑וֹר וְ֝תִקְשְׁרֶ֗נּוּ לְנַעֲרוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
30
Will guilds bargain over him? Will they divide him among the Canaanite merchants?
יִכְר֣וּ עָ֭לָיו חַבָּרִ֑ים יֶ֝חֱצ֗וּהוּ בֵּ֣ין כְּֽנַעֲנִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 30

The market-trade question. Chabbarim — companions, members of a guild (here probably fishing-guilds or merchant-associations). Kena'anim — Canaanites, but in the chapter's economic register, the Canaanite merchants — the Phoenician trading network. // The chapter is asking: can Leviathan be commodified — divided up, sold, distributed through the trade-network? The implicit answer: no, no market would handle Leviathan-as-merchandise. The chapter is staging Leviathan as outside the economic system that handles other large creatures.

31
Can you fill his hide with barbs, or his head with fish-spears?
הַֽתְמַלֵּ֣א בְשֻׂכּ֣וֹת עוֹר֑וֹ וּבְצִלְצַ֖ל דָּגִ֣ים רֹאשֽׁוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 31

Harpoon-and-spear vocabulary. Sukkot (barbed implements). Tziltzal dagim — fish-spears, fishing-tridents. The chapter is now asking: can you use the largest available marine hunting-equipment on Leviathan? Implicit answer: no.

32
Lay your hand on him — remember the battle, do not repeat it.
שִׂים־עָלָ֥יו כַּפֶּ֑ךָ זְכֹ֥ר מִ֝לְחָמָ֗ה אַל־תּוֹסַֽף׃
Translation Commentary Verse 32

The chapter's closing warning. Sim alav kapekha z'khor milchamah al-tosaf — lay your hand on him; remember the battle, do not repeat it. The chapter is staging the warning as post-attempt advice: if you have ever tried to lay hands on Leviathan, you remember the fight — and you will not try again. // The chapter ends here (Hebrew Job 40 chapter division); Hebrew Job 41 will continue with the full Leviathan description (his shield-scales, his sneezes-of-light, the fire from his mouth, his impenetrable defenses).

Chapter 41

Job 41 — Leviathan, king over all the proud

1
Behold, the hope of him is disappointed — even at the sight of him is one cast down?
Call now; is there any that will answer thee? And to which of the holy ones wilt thou turn?
הֵן־תֹּחַלְתּ֥וֹ נִכְזָ֑בָה הֲגַ֖ם אֶל־מַרְאָ֣יו יֻטָֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The chapter opens on capture-disappointment. Tochalto nikhzavah — the hope of him is disappointed. Anyone who hoped to subdue Leviathan finds his expectation thwarted. Ha-gam el-mar'av yutal — is one even at the sight of him cast down? The mere visual presence of Leviathan is overwhelming. // The verse continues the unsubdueability argument from chapter 40. The English chapter division (where this verse is Job 41:9) breaks the continuity that the Hebrew preserves — the Leviathan description flows from 40:25 to 41:26 in the Hebrew without chapter-boundary, but the English tradition splits 41:1 at what is 40:25 in Hebrew and starts 41:9 (English) here at Hebrew 41:1.

2
None is so fierce that they would dare rouse him — and who is he that can stand before me?
For vexation killeth the foolish man, And jealousy slayeth the silly one.
לֹֽא־אַ֭כְזָר כִּ֣י יְעוּרֶ֑נּוּ וּמִ֥י ה֝֗וּא לְפָנַ֥י יִתְיַצָּֽב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The pivot from Leviathan to YHWH. The verse turns on a remarkable rhetorical structure: no one is fierce enough to rouse Leviathan ... and who can stand before me? The chapter is making an a-fortiori argument: if no one can take on Leviathan, certainly no one can take on Leviathan's maker. The chapter has staged Leviathan as the most-fearsome accessible creature — and uses that fear-status to magnify YHWH's status. // The verb yityatzav (stand before, present oneself before) is the same form as in Job 1:6, 2:1 (the bnei ha-elohim presenting themselves before YHWH; see mit'yatzev-al-YHWH). The chapter is invoking the council-presentation register — but now in inverted form: who could stand before YHWH in the adversarial-litigation sense Job has been attempting?

3
Who has come before me, that I should repay? Under all the skies, it is mine.
I have seen the foolish taking root: But suddenly I cursed his habitation.
מִ֣י הִ֭קְדִּימַנִי וַאֲשַׁלֵּ֑ם תַּ֖חַת כָּל־הַשָּׁמַ֣יִם לִי־הֽוּא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The ownership-claim. Tachat kol ha-shamayim li-hu — under all the skies, it is mine. The chapter is staking the comprehensive cosmic ownership claim: everything under the heavens belongs to YHWH. The verse interrupts the Leviathan description with this brief assertion before returning to the description.

4
I will not keep silent about his limbs, or his mighty deeds, or the grace of his proportion.
His children are far from safety, And they are crushed in the gate, Neither is there any to deliver them:
לא־[לֽוֹ־] אַחֲרִ֥ישׁ בַּדָּ֑יו וּדְבַר־גְּ֝בוּר֗וֹת וְחִ֣ין עֶרְכּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The chapter announces its descriptive mission. Lo achrish badav — I will not keep silent about his limbs. The chapter is going to describe Leviathan in detail — limbs, deeds, proportions. The next 22 verses (vv 5-26) are sustained physical description. // Ketiv/qere note: lo (qere) for l' (ketiv) — the difference is to him (ketiv) vs. not (qere). The qere is the standard reading: 'I will not keep silent'.

5
Who has unveiled the face of his garment? Into the double of his bridle, who shall come?
Whose harvest the hungry eateth up, And taketh it even out of the thorns; And the snare gapeth for their substance.
מִֽי־גִ֭לָּה פְּנֵ֣י לְבוּשׁ֑וֹ בְּכֶ֥פֶל רִ֝סְנ֗וֹ מִ֣י יָבֽוֹא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Leviathan's outer covering. Penei l'vusho — the face of his garment. Leviathan has a l'vush — a garment, outer covering — and a resen — a bridle, double-layer mouth-restraint. The chapter is describing his outer surface as a layered defensive armor, with the next verses (vv 6-9) detailing the scale-system.

6
Who can open the doors of his face? Around his teeth is terror.
For affliction cometh not forth from the dust, Neither doth trouble spring out of the ground;
דַּלְתֵ֣י פָ֭נָיו מִ֣י פִתֵּ֑חַ סְבִיב֖וֹת שִׁנָּ֣יו אֵימָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Doors-of-his-face and teeth-of-terror. Daltei panav — doors of his face — names the jaws as closable doors. The architectural-mechanical register (daltayim — doors) recurs from Job 38:8-10 (the sea-restraint doors). The same vocabulary describes Leviathan's anatomy: his jaws are gates. // Aimah — terror, dread — surrounds his teeth. The terror is not just an effect on observers but is circumambient to the teeth themselves — a property of the creature, not just a response in the observer.

7
His pride is the channels of shields, shut up as with a tight seal.
But man is born unto trouble, As the sparks fly upward.
גַּ֭אֲוָה אֲפִיקֵ֣י מָֽגִנִּ֑ים סָ֝ג֗וּר חוֹתָ֥ם צָֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The shield-scales. Afiqei maginnim — channels of shields. The chapter is naming Leviathan's scales as rows of shields: armor-plates arranged in channels along his body. Sagur chotam tzar — shut up as with a tight seal. The seal-metaphor names the interlocking tightness of the scale-plate system. // The image is consistent with the heavy armor-scales of crocodilians (the standard scholarly identification for Leviathan is the Nile crocodile). Each scale is heavy keratinous bone-backed plate; the arrangement in rows along the back creates an interlocking armored surface. The Hebrew vocabulary (shield, seal) is precise to this anatomy.

8
One to another they touch — and no breath comes between them.
But as for me, I would seek unto God, And unto God would I commit my cause;
אֶחָ֣ד בְּאֶחָ֣ד יִגַּ֑שׁוּ וְ֝ר֗וּחַ לֹא־יָב֥וֹא בֵֽינֵיהֶֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The scale-tightness specified. Echad b-echad yiggashu — one to one they touch. V-ruach lo yavo beneihem — and no breath comes between them. // The chapter is naming the air-tight interlocking of the scale-armor: not even ruach (wind, breath) penetrates between the scales. The image is precision-engineered armor. The verbal echo of ruach (the same noun as in ch 1:2's ruach Elohim, in the chayyot's ruach-chayyah, and throughout Job) is part of the lens-relevant vocabulary cluster. Leviathan's armor is impenetrable even to the ruach.

9
Each cleaves to its brother — they are joined and cannot be separated.
Who doeth great things and unsearchable, Marvellous things without number:
אִישׁ־בְּאָחִ֥יהוּ יְדֻבָּ֑קוּ יִ֝תְלַכְּד֗וּ וְלֹ֣א יִתְפָּרָֽדוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The scale-fusion. Ish b-achihu y'dubaqu — each cleaves to its brother. Yitlakkadu v-lo yitparadu — they are joined and cannot be separated. The chapter completes the scale-armor description: the scales are fused, locked together, inseparable. The armor-system is single-piece in operation, multi-scale in structure.

10
His sneezes flash light, and his eyes are like the eyelids of dawn.
Who giveth rain upon the earth, And sendeth waters upon the fields;
עֲ‍ֽ֭טִישֹׁתָיו תָּ֣הֶל א֑וֹר וְ֝עֵינָ֗יו כְּעַפְעַפֵּי־שָֽׁחַר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Light-emitting sneezes. Atishotav tahel or — his sneezes flash light. The chapter is naming Leviathan as a creature whose sneezes emit light — a phenomenon the chapter does not explain but names directly. // The image has been read in multiple ways: (1) sun-reflection — sneeze-spray catches sunlight at certain angles, producing rainbow / refraction effects (the standard naturalistic reading for the crocodile identification); (2) bioluminescence-like phenomenon — some kind of inherent light-emission (lens-relevant; matches the dragon-tradition's fire-breathing imagery in adjacent verses); (3) mythological-cosmological (Leviathan as the cosmic-sea creature whose body-functions are themselves cosmic-phenomena). // Einav k-af'apei shachar — his eyes are like the eyelids of dawn. The same image is reused in Egyptian hieroglyphics for the crocodile-eye motif (the Heru-eye / Sun-eye connection). The chapter is invoking iconographic-symbolic eye-imagery — the dawn-eye signifying first-light, watchful presence. The Babylonian Talmud (b. Berakhot 3a) takes this verse as the etymological source of the morning-eye motif in Jewish liturgy.

11
From his mouth go forth torches; sparks of fire shoot out.
So that he setteth up on high those that are low, And those that mourn are exalted to safety.
מִ֭פִּיו לַפִּידִ֣ים יַהֲלֹ֑כוּ כִּיד֥וֹדֵי אֵ֝֗שׁ יִתְמַלָּֽטוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Mipiv lappidim yahalokhu — from his mouth go forth torches. Kidodei esh yitmalatu — sparks of fire shoot out. // This is the chapter's most-iconic Leviathan image: fire from the mouth. The vocabulary is precise: lappidim (torches — burning brands of fire) and kidodei esh (sparks of fire — embers, glowing fragments). The chapter is describing Leviathan as a fire-breathing creature. // The image is direct: Leviathan emits fire from his mouth. The standard scholarly readings have proposed: (1) mythological-poetic (the canonical Hebrew Bible dragon-tradition image, paralleled in Ugaritic, Mesopotamian, and broader ANE dragon-iconography); (2) crocodilian breath-misting (in cold morning air, the crocodile's warm breath produces visible vapor that looks like smoke/fire — but the lappidim / kidodei esh vocabulary is stronger than just vapor); (3) literal fire-emission (the chapter's plain reading; no known biological mechanism matches but the chapter's account is unambiguous). // The Wheel of Heaven lens reads this passage in continuity with: Genesis 1:21's tanninim (great dragons, taninim glossary entry, v2.0.0); Isaiah 27:1's identification of Leviathan with the tannin in the sea; the global dragon-tradition's fire-breathing motif (European dragons, Mesopotamian Tiamat, Chinese long); and the canonical fire-from-mouth imagery in apocalyptic-cosmic-battle texts (Rev 11:5, Rev 12 of the dragon). The chapter is staging Leviathan as the canonical fire-breathing sea-dragon of the Hebrew Bible's cosmic-creature vocabulary.

12
From his nostrils smoke goes out, like a boiling pot blown on, and the rush.
He frustrateth the devices of the crafty, So that their hands cannot perform their enterprise.
מִ֭נְּחִירָיו יֵצֵ֣א עָשָׁ֑ן כְּד֖וּד נָפ֣וּחַ וְאַגְמֹֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

Smoke from nostrils. Mi-n'chirav yetze ashan — from his nostrils smoke goes out. The fire-mouth (v 11) and the smoke-nostrils (v 12) together complete the canonical dragon-fire-and-smoke imagery. // K-dud nafuach v-agmon — like a boiling pot blown on, and the rush. The boiling-pot imagery names the roaring-vapor quality of the smoke emission. The reading is mechanical-thermal: Leviathan's internal heat is so intense that smoke pours from his nostrils as from a kettle being blown to flame.

13
His breath kindles coals, and flame goes out of his mouth.
He taketh the wise in their own craftiness; And the counsel of the cunning is carried headlong.
נַ֭פְשׁוֹ גֶּחָלִ֣ים תְּלַהֵ֑ט וְ֝לַ֗הַב מִפִּ֥יו יֵצֵֽא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The fire-source is the nefesh. Nafsho gechalim t'lahet — his breath kindles coals. The chapter is locating the fire-source in Leviathan's nefesh (breath, life-soul) — the same word as in Gen 2:7 (the human becomes a nefesh chayyah — living being). // The verse is the chapter's clearest statement of Leviathan's fire-emission mechanism: his nefesh itself burns; coals catch fire from his breath; flame issues from his mouth. The phenomenon is internal to the creature, not external (he doesn't have to be lit; he is on fire from within).

14
In his neck dwells strength, and dismay leaps before his face.
They meet with darkness in the day-time, And grope at noonday as in the night.
בְּֽ֭צַוָּארוֹ יָלִ֣ין עֹ֑ז וּ֝לְפָנָ֗יו תָּד֥וּץ דְּאָבָֽה׃
15
The folds of his flesh cleave together — cast firm upon him, they do not move.
But he saveth from the sword of their mouth, Even the needy from the hand of the mighty.
מַפְּלֵ֣י בְשָׂר֣וֹ דָבֵ֑קוּ יָצ֥וּק עָ֝לָ֗יו בַּל־יִמּֽוֹט׃
16
His heart is cast firm as stone — yes, cast firm as a millstone's lower part.
So the poor hath hope, And iniquity stoppeth her mouth.
לִ֭בּוֹ יָצ֣וּק כְּמוֹ־אָ֑בֶן וְ֝יָצ֗וּק כְּפֶ֣לַח תַּחְתִּֽית׃
17
At his rising, mighty ones fear; at the crashings, they purify themselves.
Behold, happy is the man whom God correcteth: Therefore despise not thou the chastening of the Almighty.
מִ֭שֵּׂתוֹ יָג֣וּרוּ אֵלִ֑ים מִ֝שְּׁבָרִ֗ים יִתְחַטָּֽאוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The mighty ones fear. Yaguru elim — mighty ones fear. Elim is plural of el — mighty ones, divine beings. The word is the same root as Elohim, El Shaddai, Elohei Yisrael. The chapter is naming even divine-class beings as fearing at Leviathan's rising. // The lens reads this with attention: Leviathan is fearsome even to the divine-class beings (the elim, possibly the same class as the bnei ha-elohim of 1:6, 2:1, 38:7). The chapter is positioning Leviathan as a creature that gives pause to the council itself. The combined fire-and-cosmic-fear register is consistent with the apocalyptic-dragon tradition (Rev 12:7-9 the dragon making war with the angels).

18
If one attacks him with the sword, it will not stand — nor the spear, the dart, or the breastplate.
For he maketh sore, and bindeth up; He woundeth, and his hands make whole.
מַשִּׂיגֵ֣הוּ חֶ֭רֶב בְּלִ֣י תָק֑וּם חֲנִ֖ית מַסָּ֣ע וְשִׁרְיָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Weapon-resistance catalog. Cherev (sword), chanit (spear), massa (dart, javelin), shiryah (breastplate / shirt-of-mail). The chapter is naming the standard military armaments as ineffective against Leviathan. None of these weapons stand against him.

19
He counts iron as straw, bronze as rotten wood.
He will deliver thee in six troubles; Yea, in seven there shall no evil touch thee.
יַחְשֹׁ֣ב לְתֶ֣בֶן בַּרְזֶ֑ל לְעֵ֖ץ רִקָּב֣וֹן נְחוּשָֽׁה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

Metallurgical-strength inversion. Barzel (iron) is counted as teven (straw). Nechushah (bronze) is counted as etz riqavon (rotten wood). The chapter is inverting the canonical Hebrew Bible metal-strength hierarchy: what humans consider hardest (iron) is straw to Leviathan; what humans consider firm (bronze) is rotten wood. // The verse establishes Leviathan as a creature whose physical strength exceeds the entire human metallurgical-weapons-armor system. The same iron-and-bronze that Behemoth had for bones (40:18 — bones as bronze channels, limbs as iron bars) is what Leviathan counts as nothing. The two creatures together are positioned as beyond the human metallurgical-civilization frame.

20
The arrow does not make him flee; sling-stones are turned to chaff for him.
In famine he will redeem thee from death; And in war from the power of the sword.
לֹֽא־יַבְרִיחֶ֥נּוּ בֶן־קָ֑שֶׁת לְ֝קַ֗שׁ נֶהְפְּכוּ־ל֥וֹ אַבְנֵי־קָֽלַע׃
21
Clubs are counted as chaff — he laughs at the rattling of the javelin.
Thou shalt be hid from the scourge of the tongue; Neither shalt thou be afraid of destruction when it cometh.
כְּ֭קַשׁ נֶחְשְׁב֣וּ תוֹתָ֑ח וְ֝יִשְׂחַ֗ק לְרַ֣עַשׁ כִּידֽוֹן׃
22
Underneath him are sharp potsherds; he spreads as a threshing-sledge upon the mud.
At destruction and dearth thou shalt laugh; Neither shalt thou be afraid of the beasts of the earth.
תַּ֭חְתָּיו חַדּ֣וּדֵי חָ֑רֶשׂ יִרְפַּ֖ד חָר֣וּץ עֲלֵי־טִֽיט׃
23
He makes the deep boil like a pot; he stirs the sea like a perfumer's mixing-bowl.
For thou shalt be in league with the stones of the field; And the beasts of the field shall be at peace with thee.
יַרְתִּ֣יחַ כַּסִּ֣יר מְצוּלָ֑ה יָ֝֗ם יָשִׂ֥ים כַּמֶּרְקָחָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

Leviathan agitates the deep. Yartiach ka-sir m'tzulah — he makes the deep boil like a pot. Yam yasim ka-mirqachah — he stirs the sea like a perfumer's mixing-bowl. // M'tzulah (depth, abyss) and yam (sea) name the deep-water domain as Leviathan's habitat. He stirs the sea like a pot being boiled — a thermal-mechanical image. The chapter is staging Leviathan as a creature whose movements affect the deep-water domain on cosmic scale — when Leviathan moves, the sea itself boils.

24
He leaves a shining path behind him — one would think the deep had grey hair.
And thou shalt know that thy tent is in peace; And thou shalt visit thy fold, and shalt miss nothing.
אַ֭חֲרָיו יָאִ֣יר נָתִ֑יב יַחְשֹׁ֖ב תְּה֣וֹם לְשֵׂיבָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

The wake. Acharav ya'ir nativ — behind him a path shines. The verb ya'ir — gives light, makes shine — names the luminous wake Leviathan leaves as he moves through the deep. // Yachshov tehom l-seyvah — one would think the deep had grey hair. Seyvah (grey hair, white hair, age-whiteness) is used as a metaphor for the white-foaming wake behind the moving creature: the deep looks aged, white-haired, in Leviathan's wake. // The verb yachshov (think, reckon, compute) is poetic-attributive: one would calculate / reckon the deep to have grey hair. The chapter is using a bioluminescence-or-foam image for the visible wake. The reading is striking — Leviathan leaves a luminous trail through the deep. // Tehom is the same primordial-deep word as Gen 1:2 (see tehom). The chapter's tehom is Leviathan's territory.

25
There is no rule of him upon the dust — he was made without fear.
Thou shalt know also that thy seed shall be great, And thine offspring as the grass of the earth.
אֵֽין־עַל־עָפָ֥ר מָשְׁל֑וֹ הֶ֝עָשׂ֗וּ לִבְלִי־חָֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

No earthly counterpart. Ein al-afar mosh'lo — there is no rule of him upon the dust. Afar (dust) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for the terrestrial human-creaturely domain (Gen 2:7 — humanity formed from the dust of the ground). The chapter is asserting that on the terrestrial dust-domain, there is nothing like Leviathan, nothing that rules in his manner. // He'asu li-vli chat — he was made without fear. The chapter names Leviathan as the canonical fearless creature — created without the capacity for fear that all earthly creatures (including humans) have. The verse closes the description with the singular-class affirmation: Leviathan is without equal among created beings.

26
He sees everything that is high; he is king over all the sons of pride.
Thou shalt come to thy grave in a full age, Like as a shock of grain cometh in in its season.
אֵֽת־כָּל־גָּבֹ֥הַּ יִרְאֶ֑ה ה֝֗וּא מֶ֣לֶךְ עַל־כָּל־בְּנֵי־שָֽׁחַץ׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 26

The closing identification: King over all the sons of pride. Hu melekh al kol bnei shachatz — he is king over all the sons of pride. // Bnei shachatz — sons of pride — is a hapax-style compound (occurring only here in this specific form). Shachatz (שָׁחַץ) is rare; the same noun appears at Job 28:8 (bnei shachatz — sons of the proud, parallel to wild beasts). The compound names a class of proud / lofty / wild creatures. // The chapter closes Leviathan's description by declaring him king of the class. The structural-rhetorical close is significant: the chapter's second divine speech opened (40:9-14) with a challenge for Job to humble all that is proud (ge'eh, gei'im). Now the chapter closes with Leviathan as king over all proud creatures — the exact category Job was challenged to subdue. The frame is closed: Job cannot subdue the pride-class because Leviathan, who is king of that class, is unsubdueable by Job. // The structural connection from 40:11-13 (the challenge to humble the proud) to 41:26 (Leviathan as king of the proud) is the chapter's keystone argument: humbling the proud is divine work, not human work — because the king of the proud is a creature only the creator can subdue. The chapter has staged Leviathan as the proof-by-example of the impossibility of human-cosmic-justice-administration. // The divine speeches are now structurally complete. Chapter 42 will record Job's full response (vv 1-6) and the restoration epilogue (vv 7-17).

Chapter 42

Job 42 — Job's submission and the restoration epilogue

1
Then Job answered YHWH and said:
Then Job answered and said,
וַיַּ֖עַן אִיּ֥וֹב אֶת־יְהוָ֗ה וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
2
I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be withheld.
Oh that my vexation were but weighed, And all my calamity laid in the balances!
ידעת [יָ֭דַעְתִּי] כִּי־כֹ֣ל תּוּכָ֑ל וְלֹא־יִבָּצֵ֖ר מִמְּךָ֣ מְזִמָּֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Ketiv / qere. The written form (ketiv) is yada'ta — you have known — addressed to YHWH (Job acknowledging YHWH's knowledge). The read form (qere) is yada'ti — I have known / I know (Job acknowledging his own coming-to-know). The translation follows the qere per the standing rule and the consensus of modern translations (ASV, NRSV, NJPS, Newsom). The ketiv is a defensible minority — it would frame v 2 as Job affirming YHWH's omniscience before turning to his own ignorance in v 3 — but the qere reading (Job declaring his own changed knowledge) connects more cleanly to the keystone of v 5 (now my eye has seen you).

The yibatzer echo of Gen 11:6. Lo yibatzer mimkha mezimmah — no purpose of yours can be withheld — uses the same passive verb (niphal of batsar) and the same negated-impossibility construction as YHWH's own assessment of human coordination at Babel (Gen 11:6: lo yibatzer mehem kol asher yazmu la'asot — nothing they have schemed to do will be withheld from them). The structural inversion is precise: Babel-humans' schemes (zamemu) cannot be withheld from them; YHWH's purpose (mezimmah) cannot be withheld from him. The noun mezimmah shares the root with the Babel verb. Job is acknowledging — by deliberate verbal echo — that the same engineering-assessment YHWH applied to human capacity at Babel applies absolutely to YHWH's own. The book closes its arc by quoting the Tower-of-Babel verdict back at YHWH and submitting to it.

3
'Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge?' Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand — things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.
For now it would be heavier than the sand of the seas: Therefore have my words been rash.
מִ֤י זֶ֨ה ׀ מַעְלִ֥ים עֵצָ֗ה בְּֽלִ֫י דָ֥עַת לָכֵ֣ן הִ֭גַּדְתִּי וְלֹ֣א אָבִ֑ין נִפְלָא֥וֹת מִ֝מֶּ֗נִּי וְלֹ֣א אֵדָֽע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Job quotes YHWH back at himself. The opening clause is a verbatim citation of YHWH's challenge at 38:2 (mi zeh ma'lim etzah b-li da'at — who is this that hides counsel without knowledge). Job's response-strategy in this submission speech is to take YHWH's own opening words and apply them to himself: yes, I was that one. The single-quote marking in the English is the WoH project's convention for embedded self-citation; the Hebrew does not mark quotation, but the verbal repetition of 38:2 leaves no doubt that the clause is being cited rather than asserted afresh. The same self-citation move recurs in v 4 with YHWH's challenge formula from 38:3 / 40:7.

Niphla'ot mimmenni — things too wonderful for me. The preposition min with niphla'ot admits two readings: wonders from me (things I produced — implausible in context) or wonders too great for me (things beyond my comprehension). The latter is standard (ASV, KJV, NRSV, Newsom).

4
'Hear, I pray, and I will speak; I will ask of you, and you make me know.'
For the arrows of the Almighty are within me, The poison whereof my spirit drinketh up: The terrors of God do set themselves in array against me.
שְֽׁמַֽע־נָ֭א וְאָנֹכִ֣י אֲדַבֵּ֑ר אֶ֝שְׁאָלְךָ֗ וְהוֹדִיעֵֽנִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Second self-citation. This is YHWH's challenge-formula from 38:3 and 40:7 (I will ask you, and you make me know), here turned around: in the divine speeches YHWH is the one who will ask and Job is the one who must make known. Job repeats the formula in the same direction (I will ask, you make me know) — accepting the asymmetry the speeches established and signaling that he is now the one positioned to receive instruction, not to demand it. The role-reversal is structural to the closing speech.

5
By the hearing of the ear I had heard of you, but now my eye has seen you.
Doth the wild ass bray when he hath grass? Or loweth the ox over his fodder?
לְשֵֽׁמַע־אֹ֥זֶן שְׁמַעְתִּ֑יךָ וְ֝עַתָּ֗ה עֵינִ֥י רָאָֽתְךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The keystone verse of the book. The chiastic contrast is exact: l-shema ozen shmati-kha / v-atah eyni ra'at-kha — by the hearing of the ear I had heard of you / and now my eye has seen you. Two clauses, four nouns: hearing-ear against eye-seeing, heard-of-you against seen-you. The Hebrew is patterned: each clause carries a sense-organ noun (ozen — ear, eyin — eye), a verb of perception in its register (shama — hear, ra'ah — see), and YHWH as the object of perception (the 2ms suffix -kha).

The contrast is between hearsay-knowledge and direct-experiential knowledge. Shema ozen — the hearing of the ear — is the canonical Hebrew Bible idiom for report-knowledge (cf. Ps 18:45, l-shema ozen yishame'u li — at the hearing of the ear they submit to me). The expression names knowledge that comes through transmitted speech rather than through direct encounter. Eyin ra'ata-kha — my eye has seen you — names the inverse: direct visual contact. The book's theological climax is exactly this transition: Job had heard about YHWH, in the canonical Wisdom-tradition register the friends operate in; now Job has seen YHWH, in the prophetic-encounter register that the whirlwind speeches inaugurated.

The verse is the book's argument for direct-encounter epistemology. The friends' position throughout chs 3–37 is the shema ozen position: knowledge of YHWH is transmitted through the wisdom-tradition, and Job should submit to that traditional knowledge. Job's position is that the tradition has failed in his case, and that he needs YHWH's own response. The whirlwind speeches provide it. The keystone verse names the epistemological shift: Job has been moved from secondhand to firsthand knowledge of his interlocutor.

The translation choice. Modern translations diverge on register. ASV and KJV preserve the chiastic structure literally (by the hearing of the ear); NRSV smooths to I had heard of you by hearsay; Newsom's NIB renders by report of the ear. The WoH translation follows ASV's preservation because the verbal patterning is itself part of the verse's argument — the parallel construction of the two sense-modalities is what makes the contrast load-bearing. Smoothing to hearsay in English loses the ozen (ear) noun that pairs with eyin (eye); the chiasm is the meaning. Newsom 2003 (NIB) explicitly defends preserving the chiastic structure on the same ground.

The lens stays quiet here. This verse is among the most-edited and least-disputed lines in the book. The WoH lens does not need to do interpretive work on it: Job 42:5 is wisdom-literature in the international ANE register, and the direct-encounter / hearsay distinction is the book's own theology, not a project-specific reading.

6
Therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes.
Can that which hath no savor be eaten without salt? Or is there any taste in the white of an egg?
עַל־כֵּ֭ן אֶמְאַ֣ס וְנִחַ֑מְתִּי עַל־עָפָ֥ר וָאֵֽפֶר׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The most-disputed verse in Job, and one of the most-disputed in the entire Hebrew Bible. Six words: al-ken em'as v-nikhamti al afar va-efer. Every word is contested.

Lexical underdetermination. (1) Em'as (אֶמְאַס) — I despise / reject / retract / melt away. The verb is intransitive in form here; no explicit object is given. Traditional translation supplies myself ("I despise myself"); modern philology (Newsom 1996, 2003; Curtis 1979; Clines WBC 2011) reads it as intransitive — I retract (my previous speech), I melt away (in submission), or simply I reject without specified object. (2) Nikhamti (נִחַמְתִּי) — niphal of nacham, whose semantic range covers be sorry, repent, change one's mind, be comforted, console oneself. The verb cluster is notorious in HB philology (cf. Gen 6:6, vayinachem YHWH — and YHWH was sorry / changed mind; glossary entry vayinnachem-yhwh). (3) Al afar va-efer — the preposition al with afar va-efer (dust and ashes) admits both the locative reading (in dust and ashes, i.e., Job sitting in his ash-heap repenting) and the prepositional-object reading (concerning dust and ashes, i.e., Job retracting his ash-heap-mourning posture, or expressing comfort about his human condition).

The three major modern readings.

(a) Traditional submission (ASV, KJV, the medieval Jewish commentators, most Christian theological readings): Therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes. Job has been brought to recognize his sin (or his presumption), and he submits in the canonical ash-heap-mourning posture. The book becomes a vindication of submission to divine sovereignty.

(b) Newsom's retract-the-lament-posture (Newsom NIB 1996, 2003 — the leading modern critical reading): Therefore I despise myself and repent of dust and ashes. The al is read as concerning. Job is not submitting to YHWH — he is retracting the mourning-posture itself, releasing the ash-heap stance. He has moved past needing to mourn; the direct encounter has rendered the lament-posture obsolete. The book becomes a vindication of Job's complaint (since Job is not made to apologize for his speech, only to release his mourning).

(c) Curtis 1979 / Patrick 1976 — comfort without repentance: Therefore I retract, and I am comforted, concerning dust and ashes. Em'as is intransitive ("I retract"); nikhamti is read in its be-comforted sense (Hebrew nacham in the niphal commonly means be comforted, cf. Gen 24:67, 37:35, 38:12, 2 Sam 13:39, Ezek 14:22). Job declares himself comforted concerning his human condition — dust and ashes (a stock phrase for mortal-frailty, cf. Gen 18:27 anokhi afar va-efer — Abraham at Mamre). No self-rejection, no repentance, only release.

The choice is theologically load-bearing. Reading (a) makes the book end with Job's submission; readings (b) and (c) make it end with Job's vindication. The Hebrew syntax supports all three; the question is which the book's own argument demands.

The chosen reading. The WoH translation retains ASV's traditional rendering (Therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes) in the English text, against the project's general preference for modern critical readings, on the following grounds. First, the standing rule is that where the source is genuinely underdetermined, the translation defaults to the most-attested public-domain rendering and surfaces the alternatives in commentary; that rule applies maximally here. Second, the al afar va-efer phrase echoes the same words in 2:8 (where Job sits b-tokh ha-efer — in the midst of the ash-heap) and the verbal-physical-posture reading has the strongest contextual support: Job is, at the close, still in the dust-and-ashes setting he entered the dialogue in. Third, the WoH lens is not invested in either reading: the book of Job is wisdom-literature, not a divine-council-disclosure text, and the keystone of the closing is v 5 (the direct-encounter epistemology), not v 6 (the ambiguous submission/release). The lens stays quiet here.

Where the lens does have a small remark. Reading (c) — I retract, and I am comforted, concerning dust and ashes — has a faint connection to the book's framing problem. Job 1:21 (YHWH gave, YHWH took) and Job 2:10 (shall we accept good from the Elohim and not the bad) established Job's stance as one of acceptance-of-the-given without the friends' explanatory machinery. Reading (c) would make Job's closing posture continuous with his opening posture: comforted, not repentant. Reading (a) breaks that continuity by introducing self-rejection that the book has not previously demanded. The WoH lens registers this asymmetry as a reason the modern critical readings (b, c) deserve serious weight — but does not adjust the translation, since the philological dispute is properly resolved by Hebrew Bible specialists and not by the WoH project's interpretive needs.

7
And it came to pass, after YHWH had spoken these words to Job, that YHWH said to Eliphaz the Temanite: My anger burns against you and against your two companions, for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.
My soul refuseth to touch [them]; They are as loathsome food to me.
וַיְהִ֗י אַחַ֨ר דִּבֶּ֧ר יְהוָ֛ה אֶת־הַדְּבָרִ֥ים הָאֵ֖לֶּה אֶל־אִיּ֑וֹב וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־אֱלִיפַ֣ז הַתֵּֽימָנִ֗י חָרָ֨ה אַפִּ֤י בְךָ֙ וּבִשְׁנֵ֣י רֵעֶ֔יךָ כִּ֠י לֹ֣א דִבַּרְתֶּ֥ם אֵלַ֛י נְכוֹנָ֖ה כְּעַבְדִּ֥י אִיּֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The verdict, and the keystone inversion. YHWH addresses Eliphaz — the senior friend, the first speaker in the dialogue cycle — and declares: lo dibartem elay nekhonah k-avdi Iyov — you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has. The verdict is the book's keystone inversion: the friends, who throughout chs 3–37 defended divine justice and accused Job of hidden sin, are condemned; Job, who throughout chs 3–37 accused YHWH of injustice and demanded an accounting, is vindicated.

Elay — "of me" or "to me"? The preposition el normally takes a directional sense (to, toward); the expected referential preposition would be al (about, concerning). Here el is being used referentially — about / of me. All major translations (ASV, KJV, NRSV, Newsom) render of me. Some commentators (Habel OTL 1985) keep the directional sense — you have not spoken to me what is right — which would mean you addressed me but did not address me with rectitude. The directional reading is grammatically tighter but contextually awkward; the referential reading is what the verdict-context demands. The WoH translation follows the standard of me.

Nekhonah — what is right / established / correct. The feminine singular adjective is used substantivally: the right thing. The verb kun (to be established, firm) gives the underlying sense of what stands, what is established as true. The friends' speeches were eloquent but did not stand as true accounts of YHWH; Job's speeches — including his accusations — did.

The lens's small note. The book's framing chapters (1–2) staged Job's affliction as the result of a bnei-elohim council scene, with ha-satan as adversarial-prosecutor (see entries b-nei-ha-elohim, ha-satan, mit'yatzev-al-YHWH). The dialogue cycle (chs 3–37) and the divine speeches (chs 38–41) never refer back to the council scene; the friends do not know about it, and Job does not know about it. The verdict here closes the frame: the friends' theological-justification machinery was wrong precisely because the council scene's logic — Job's affliction as test, not as punishment for hidden sin — was something the wisdom-tradition's retributive-justice axiom could not see. The WoH lens does not need to push this further; the book's own framing is sufficient. The connection back to the bnei-elohim scene is the structural reason the friends are condemned: their entire defense rested on an axiom (suffering proves sin) that the framing chapters had already shown to be false.

8
Now therefore, take for yourselves seven bulls and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and offer up a burnt-offering for yourselves; and my servant Job shall pray for you — for him only will I accept, that I may not deal with you according to folly; for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.
Oh that I might have my request; And that God would grant [me] the thing that I long for!
וְעַתָּ֡ה קְחֽוּ־לָכֶ֣ם שִׁבְעָֽה־פָרִים֩ וְשִׁבְעָ֨ה אֵילִ֜ים וּלְכ֣וּ ׀ אֶל־עַבְדִּ֣י אִיּ֗וֹב וְהַעֲלִיתֶ֤ם עוֹלָה֙ בַּֽעַדְכֶ֔ם וְאִיּ֣וֹב עַבְדִּ֔י יִתְפַּלֵּ֖ל עֲלֵיכֶ֑ם כִּ֧י אִם־פָּנָ֣יו אֶשָּׂ֗א לְבִלְתִּ֞י עֲשׂ֤וֹת עִמָּכֶם֙ נְבָלָ֔ה כִּ֠י לֹ֣א דִבַּרְתֶּ֥ם אֵלַ֛י נְכוֹנָ֖ה כְּעַבְדִּ֥י אִיּֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Job becomes the intercessor. The verdict-and-remedy structure of vv 7–8 names Job not only as vindicated but as the priest-figure who must intercede for the friends — they bring the burnt-offering, but it is Job's prayer that secures YHWH's acceptance. The numerical pattern (seven bulls + seven rams) is the canonical Hebrew Bible completion-sacrifice quantity (cf. Num 23:1, Balaam's sevens; 2 Chr 29:21, Hezekiah's restoration sacrifices). The sacrifice is unusually large for an individual: this is full-restoration sacrificial volume.

Panav essa — the face-lifting idiom. Ki im panav essa — literally for I will lift his face — is the Hebrew Bible's standard idiom for acceptance / showing favor / granting petition (cf. Gen 19:21; Mal 1:8, where yissa fanekha — will he lift your face — appears in the priests' rhetorical question about the value of their offerings). The same idiom recurs in v 9 (vayyissa YHWH et-pnei Iyov — YHWH lifted/accepted the face of Job). The translation smooths to accept per ASV and the broader convention; the literal face-lifting idiom is recoverable in commentary.

Nevalah — folly, outrage, disgraceful thing. The noun nevalah (נְבָלָה) names not merely error but outrage — a grave moral-religious violation, the level of severity used for the rape of Dinah (Gen 34:7) and of Tamar (2 Sam 13:12). YHWH is saying: had I not accepted Job's intercession on your behalf, I would have dealt with you at the severity of nevalah. The threat is severe; the friends' theological error is not minor.

9
So Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went, and did as YHWH had spoken to them; and YHWH accepted the face of Job.
Even that it would please God to crush me; That he would let loose his hand, and cut me off!
וַיֵּלְכוּ֩ אֱלִיפַ֨ז הַתֵּֽימָנִ֜י וּבִלְדַּ֣ד הַשּׁוּחִ֗י צֹפַר֙ הַנַּ֣עֲמָתִ֔י וַֽיַּעֲשׂ֔וּ כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר דִּבֶּ֥ר אֲלֵיהֶ֖ם יְהוָ֑ה וַיִּשָּׂ֥א יְהוָ֖ה אֶת־פְּנֵ֥י אִיּֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The face-lifting idiom in narrative. Vayyissa YHWH et-pnei Iyov — YHWH lifted the face of Job, here in the narrator's voice. The verse closes the verdict-and-remedy unit by reporting the outcome: the friends did as commanded, the offerings were made, and YHWH accepted Job's intercession. The face-lifting idiom recurs from v 8 in its narrative-completion form.

10
And YHWH restored the fortunes of Job when he prayed on behalf of his companions; and YHWH gave Job twice as much as he had before.
And be it still my consolation, Yea, let me exult in pain that spareth not, That I have not denied the words of the Holy One.
וַֽיהוָ֗ה שָׁ֚ב אֶת־שבית [שְׁב֣וּת] אִיּ֔וֹב בְּהִֽתְפַּֽלְל֖וֹ בְּעַ֣ד רֵעֵ֑הוּ וַ֧יֹּסֶף יְהוָ֛ה אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁ֥ר לְאִיּ֖וֹב לְמִשְׁנֶֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

The pan-Hebrew-Bible restoration phrase. Shav et-sh'vut (here as shav et-sh'vit in the ketiv, sh'vut in the qere) is one of the Hebrew Bible's most-recurring formulae. The phrase appears in some 26 verses across Deuteronomy, the Major and Minor Prophets, the Psalms, and Lamentations (key occurrences: Deut 30:3; Jer 29:14, 30:3, 30:18, 31:23, 32:44, 33:7, 33:11, 33:26, 48:47, 49:6, 49:39; Ezek 16:53 [×3], 29:14, 39:25; Hos 6:11; Joel 3:1 [4:1 MT]; Amos 9:14; Zeph 2:7, 3:20; Ps 14:7, 53:7, 85:2, 126:1, 126:4; Lam 2:14; Job 42:10). The phrase is the canonical idiom for divine reversal of communal calamity — restoration after exile, after defeat, after disaster.

Two readings, modern scholarship has settled. The traditional rendering turn the captivity (ASV, KJV) takes the noun sh'vut / sh'vit from the root sh-b-h (take captive) and reads the phrase metaphorically — reverse the captivity-state. The modern scholarly consensus (Dahood 1965; Bracke JSOT 1985; HALOT, BDB qualified; Newsom NIB; NJPS 1985; NRSV) derives the noun from sh-w-b (turn, return) and reads it as restoration of fortunes — a cognate-accusative construction turn the turning meaning restore what has been lost. Bracke's 1985 study, which has become the standard reference, demonstrated philologically that the captivity-derivation is unworkable in many of the 26 occurrences (Job is the clearest case: Job was never literally taken captive, so the captivity-reading must be metaphorical from the start) and that the sh-w-b derivation gives consistent sense across all attestations.

The WoH translation departs from ASV here. Where the verse-by-verse rule is to default to the most-attested PD rendering, the shav et-shvut phrase is a case where modern scholarship has produced a settled consensus the WoH project adopts. The English text changes from turned the captivity (ASV) to restored the fortunes (NRSV / NJPS / Newsom). This is one of the rare cases in the WoH translation where the standing PD-default rule is overridden by modern-philological consensus. The glossary entry shav-et-shvut records the decision and will govern every future occurrence of the phrase in the prophets and the Psalms.

The singular re'ehu. B-hitpall'lo b-ad re'ehu — when he prayed on behalf of his companion — uses a singular noun where the context demands a plural reference (the three friends). The construction is the Hebrew collective-distributive singular (cf. Ex 14:25 vayasar — and he removed, in collective reference to the chariots; Judg 6:30 anshei ha-ir — the men of the city, in collective reference). The standard translation smooths to plural (ASV: for his friends); the WoH translation follows that smoothing as well, since the collective-distributive force is preserved in the English plural and the literal singular would mislead. The existing glossary entry shlosha-re-ei-iyov covers this collective usage.

The doubling. L-mishneh — to double / twofold. YHWH gave Job twice what he had before. The doubling applies to property (vv 12–17 list the new totals against the originals at 1:3) — but does not apply to the children: the seven sons and three daughters of v 13 match the seven sons and three daughters of 1:2 exactly, not doubled. The asymmetry is theologically deliberate: lost livestock can be replaced doubled; lost children cannot.

11
Then there came to him all his brothers and all his sisters, and all who had known him before, and they ate bread with him in his house; and they bemoaned him, and comforted him concerning all the evil that YHWH had brought upon him; and each one gave him a kesitah, and each one a ring of gold.
What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is mine end, that I should be patient?
וַיָּבֹ֣אוּ אֵ֠לָיו כָּל־אֶחָ֨יו וְכָל־אחיתיו [אַחְיוֹתָ֜יו] וְכָל־יֹדְעָ֣יו לְפָנִ֗ים וַיֹּאכְל֨וּ עִמּ֣וֹ לֶחֶם֮ בְּבֵיתוֹ֒ וַיָּנֻ֤דוּ לוֹ֙ וַיְנַחֲמ֣וּ אֹת֔וֹ עַ֚ל כָּל־הָ֣רָעָ֔ה אֲשֶׁר־הֵבִ֥יא יְהוָ֖ה עָלָ֑יו וַיִּתְּנוּ־ל֗וֹ אִ֚ישׁ קְשִׂיטָ֣ה אֶחָ֔ת וְאִ֕ישׁ נֶ֥זֶם זָהָ֖ב אֶחָֽד׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 11

Kesitah preserved. Kesitah (קְשִׂיטָה) is an archaic unit of currency or weight occurring only three times in the Hebrew Bible: here, at Gen 33:19 (Jacob's purchase of a parcel at Shechem for 100 kesitah), and at Josh 24:32 (the same Shechem land transaction, recalled). The word's etymology is uncertain — possibly from a root meaning apportioned or measured. The Septuagint at Gen 33:19 renders amnados (lamb), suggesting an early tradition of lamb-shaped weights or lamb-equivalent value, but the identification is speculative. Standard modern translation practice (Newsom, NJPS) preserves kesitah untranslated; the WoH translation follows that convention. The glossary entry kesitah will govern all three occurrences.

The narrator's verdict on the affliction. Kol ha-ra'ah asher hevi YHWH alav — all the evil that YHWH brought upon him. The narrator attributes the calamity directly to YHWH — not to ha-satan, who was the proximate agent in chs 1–2. The framing chapters and the narrator's closing align: YHWH gave, YHWH took (1:21, entry YHWH-natan-YHWH-laqach); shall we accept good from the Elohim and not bad (2:10, entry tov-v-ra-nqabel); the evil that YHWH brought upon him (here). The book's agentic attribution is consistent end-to-end: the buck stops with YHWH. Ha-satan operated under YHWH's commission and disappears from the text after 2:7; the council-scene framing does not displace YHWH's responsibility for the test-as-such.

Ra'ah preserved. The word ra'ah (רָעָה) covers the full Hebrew-Bible semantic range: evil, calamity, disaster, misfortune, harm. Modern translations sometimes soften to misfortune (NRSV) or trouble. The WoH translation keeps evil for vocabulary-consistency with 2:10 (tov v-ra — good and evil) and with the Gen 2-3 tov v-ra cluster (the tree of knowing-good-and-evil). The word is not moral evil here; it is experiential evil — calamity that has befallen Job. Preserving evil maintains the cross-corpus lexical thread.

The orthographic ketiv at achyotav. The ketiv is defectively spelled (achyotiv); the qere normalizes the orthography (achyotav — his sisters). Both yield the same meaning. The translation follows the qere; no semantic content rides on the variant.

12
And YHWH blessed the latter end of Job more than his beginning; and he had fourteen thousand sheep, and six thousand camels, and a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she-asses.
Is my strength the strength of stones? Or is my flesh of brass?
וַֽיהוָ֗ה בֵּרַ֛ךְ אֶת־אַחֲרִ֥ית אִיּ֖וֹב מֵרֵאשִׁת֑וֹ וַֽיְהִי־ל֡וֹ אַרְבָּעָה֩ עָשָׂ֨ר אֶ֜לֶף צֹ֗אן וְשֵׁ֤שֶׁת אֲלָפִים֙ גְּמַלִּ֔ים וְאֶֽלֶף־צֶ֥מֶד בָּקָ֖ר וְאֶ֥לֶף אֲתוֹנֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The doubling, itemized. Against Job's original holdings at 1:3 (seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of cattle, five hundred donkeys), the restoration totals are exactly doubled: fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, one thousand yoke of cattle, one thousand donkeys. The doubling is the canonical Hebrew Bible restitution-quantity (cf. Ex 22:4, 7, 9, where the convicted thief restores twofold; the same legal-restitution principle is applied to YHWH's restoration of Job).

13
He had also seven sons and three daughters.
Is it not that I have no help in me, And that wisdom is driven quite from me?
וַֽיְהִי־ל֛וֹ שִׁבְעָ֥נָה בָנִ֖ים וְשָׁל֥וֹשׁ בָּנֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The children are not doubled. Job's original family at 1:2 — seven sons, three daughters. The restoration: seven sons, three daughters. The same number, not doubled. The asymmetry against the property-doubling is the book's quiet theological mark: lost livestock can be returned at twofold restitution; lost children cannot be returned in that register at all.

The shiv'anah form. The numeral shiv'anah (שִׁבְעָנָה) is unusual — the standard form for seven (masculine) is shiv'ah. The form here ends with a -nah extension that some commentators (Pope AB 1965; Newsom NIB 1996) take as a dual or emphatic, suggesting an underlying twice-seven / fourteen. The minority reading would harmonize the children with the property-doubling. The grammatical-context arguments against the dual reading are stronger, however: the parallel shalosh banot (three daughters) is in the simple form, not doubled; the verb agreement is singular-collective; the rabbinic and medieval commentators uniformly read seven. The standard reading seven is retained, and the symmetry-by-numbers with 1:2 is preserved.

14
And he called the name of the first Yemimah, and the name of the second Ketziah, and the name of the third Keren-happukh.
To him that is ready to faint kindness [should be showed] from his friend; Even to him that forsaketh the fear of the Almighty.
וַיִּקְרָ֤א שֵׁם־הָֽאַחַת֙ יְמִימָ֔ה וְשֵׁ֥ם הַשֵּׁנִ֖ית קְצִיעָ֑ה וְשֵׁ֥ם הַשְּׁלִישִׁ֖ית קֶ֥רֶן הַפּֽוּךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

The three names are sense-bearing. None of Yemimah, Ketziah, Keren-happukh is attested elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible as the name of another person; all three are unique to Job's daughters. All three are also semantically transparent.

Yemimah (יְמִימָה). The name's etymology has three main proposals. (1) From yom (day) / yamim (days) — yielding day-bright, like-the-day, day-one (rabbinic; Targum Job 42:14 renders as-the-day; b. Bava Batra 16b: "as bright as the day"). (2) From yamim in its sense of warm-color (compare Arabic yam — sea, dove, warm-day) — yielding dove (Pope AB; Habel OTL; cognate with Arabic yamamah — turtle-dove). (3) From a root y-m-m of uncertain semantic content — yielding an unanalyzable proper name. The dove-reading (Newsom NIB) is currently the leading critical option.

Ketziah (קְצִיעָה). The name is identical in form to qetzi'ah — cassia, a fragrant spice related to cinnamon, attested at Ps 45:9 (qetzi'ot — cassias, in the royal wedding-psalm's perfume-list) and at Ezek 27:19 (Tyre's spice-trade inventory). The cassia-identification is universal across modern scholarship: the name is the spice.

Keren-happukh (קֶרֶן הַפּוּךְ). Literally horn of pukh. Pukh (פּוּךְ) is the dark cosmetic eye-paint / kohl / stibium-antimony used to darken eyelids — attested at 2 Kgs 9:30 (Jezebel paints her eyes ba-pukh), Jer 4:30 (the unfaithful daughter of Zion paints her eyes ba-pukh), Isa 54:11 (the stones of restored Zion set ba-pukh). Keren is horn — by extension, a horn-shaped container, the standard cosmetic-flask shape. Keren-happukh = kohl-horn, cosmetic-flask, by metaphorical extension the beautified one.

The shared semantic field. All three names cluster in beauty and fragrance and adornment: day-bright / dove, cassia-spice, kohl-flask. The naming-pattern is itself the comment v 15 will make explicit (no women so fair were found in all the land). The book closes by naming the restoration-daughters in a register that the Hebrew Bible does not normally use for genealogical purposes — daughters in Hebrew Bible patriarchal narratives are usually named (when named at all) by lineage-marker; here they are named by aesthetic attribute. The choice is structural to the book's restoration-as-beauty closing.

Translation policy. Standard practice is to preserve the names untranslated and gloss them. The WoH translation follows the project's transliteration conventions (Yemimah, Ketziah, Keren-happukh) rather than the older ASV/KJV spellings (Jemimah, Keziah, Keren-happuch). The semantic readings (Dove, Cassia, Kohl-Horn) belong in the glossary and commentary, not in the running text — preserving the names as names is the responsible default.

15
And in all the land were no women found so fair as the daughters of Job; and their father gave them an inheritance among their brothers.
My brethren have dealt deceitfully as a brook, As the channel of brooks that pass away;
וְלֹ֨א נִמְצָ֜א נָשִׁ֥ים יָפ֛וֹת כִּבְנ֥וֹת אִיּ֖וֹב בְּכָל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִּתֵּ֨ן לָהֶ֧ם אֲבִיהֶ֛ם נַחֲלָ֖ה בְּת֥וֹךְ אֲחֵיהֶֽם׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 15

The daughter-inheritance is unique in the Hebrew Bible. Standard Hebrew Bible inheritance law (Num 27:1–11, the daughters of Zelophehad case) holds that daughters inherit only when there are no surviving sons; Num 36:1–12 then constrains daughter-inheritance to require marriage within the tribe so the inheritance-portion does not transfer between tribes. Job's seven sons are alive; standard law would give the daughters no inheritance share. Job's giving the daughters an inheritance b-tokh achehem — among their brothers — is voluntary departure from the patrilineal default, and it is the Hebrew Bible's only attested case of fathers granting daughter-inheritance alongside surviving sons.

Reading 1: idealized restoration. The verse functions as part of the book's restoration-perfection imagery: in the restored Job's household, the standard patriarchal-inheritance constraints are relaxed; daughters are given the same property-share as sons. The restoration is not a return to the original state but to a better state — Job is wealthier than before (vv 12), his daughters are more beautiful than any in the land (v 15a), and his household practices a more generous inheritance norm than the surrounding law.

Reading 2: non-Israelite setting. Job is set in Uz, outside Israel; Job is not under the Mosaic inheritance code. The daughter-inheritance may simply reflect a non-Israelite Aramean / Edomite legal context where the patrilineal-default constraints of Num 27/36 do not apply. The book's geographic positioning supports this reading.

The two readings are not exclusive. The book's choice to close with the daughter-inheritance detail (the last narrative beat before the death notice) signals that the detail is load-bearing for the restoration's character, regardless of whether the underlying legal framework is Israelite-idealized or non-Israelite-actual. The book wants the reader to see the daughters fully provided for, named with sense-bearing names, regarded as beautiful in all the land. The closing is in part a closing on the status of the daughters in the restored household.

The WoH lens. The book of Job is not a divine-council-disclosure text; the lens stays quiet through most of the epilogue. The daughter-inheritance is a wisdom-literature ethical move, not a project-relevant one. It is noted for its philological-legal significance, not for any lens-specific reading.

16
And after this Job lived a hundred and forty years, and saw his sons, and his sons' sons, four generations.
Which are black by reason of the ice, [And] wherein the snow hideth itself:
וַיְחִ֤י אִיּוֹב֙ אַֽחֲרֵי־זֹ֔את מֵאָ֥ה וְאַרְבָּעִ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וירא [וַיִּרְאֶ֗ה] אֶת־בָּנָיו֙ וְאֶת־בְּנֵ֣י בָנָ֔יו אַרְבָּעָ֖ה דֹּרֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Patriarchal lifespan. 140 years after the restoration implies a total lifespan well above 140 — Job was already a household-head with grown children at the book's opening. The figure places Job in the late-patriarchal lifespan range: Abraham 175, Isaac 180, Jacob 147, Joseph 110. The Septuagint expands the verse to specify Job lived 248 years in total (70 before the affliction, 248 after — but the LXX numbers are internally inconsistent across manuscript traditions). The MT gives only the post-restoration figure; the WoH translation follows MT.

Four generations. Arba'ah dorot — four generations — is the patriarchal-blessing's canonical measure (cf. Gen 50:23, Joseph seeing the third generation; Ex 20:5/34:7, the four-generations formula in the Decalogue). Job sees his children, his children's children, and onward to four generations — the maximum a single life-witness can attain in the Hebrew Bible's standard reckoning.

17
So Job died, old and full of days.
What time they wax warm, they vanish; When it is hot, they are consumed out of their place.
וַיָּ֣מָת אִיּ֔וֹב זָקֵ֖ן וּשְׂבַ֥ע יָמִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The patriarchal-good-death formula. Zaqen u-s'va yamim — old and full of days — is the Hebrew Bible's canonical formula for the blessed completion of a long life. It is used of Abraham (Gen 25:8: vayyamot Avraham b-seyvah tovah, zaqen v-save'a), Isaac (Gen 35:29: vayyigva Yitzchaq vayyamot... zaqen u-s'va yamim), David (1 Chr 29:28: vayyamot b-seyvah tovah, s'va yamim), Jehoiada the priest (2 Chr 24:15: vayyizqan Yehoyada vayyisba yamim vayyamot), and Job (here). The formula signifies death at the natural conclusion of a full life — not violent, not premature, not under divine displeasure. The closing places Job in the lineage of the patriarchs by the canonical death-notice's own diction.

The book closes by patriarchal alignment. Job — non-Israelite, outside the covenantal line, framed by a divine-council scene rather than by a covenantal moment — is nonetheless granted the patriarchal-good-death formula. The closing is structural: the book that opened with Job placed outside Israel in the international Wisdom-tradition register closes by giving him the death-notice of an Israelite patriarch.

The LXX appendix. The Septuagint of Job adds a substantial appendix (LXX Job 42:17a–17e) after this verse, not present in any Hebrew tradition. The appendix identifies Job with Jobab king of Edom (Gen 36:33, in the genealogy of Esau's descendants), gives Job's lineage as fifth from Abraham through Esau, names his wife as an Arabian woman, and identifies his three friends as kings of Edomite peoples. The identification is ancient (likely 2nd–1st century BCE Alexandrian Jewish tradition), is found in the Testament of Job (1st century CE), and was transmitted into early Christian commentary on Job. The MT does not have it. The WoH translation follows the MT and omits the appendix; the identification is noted here as a known textual-tradition divergence between MT and LXX, with implications for the book's canonical positioning — the LXX places Job firmly in the Esau / Edomite-wisdom line, which is consistent with the geographic and cultural setting Job 1:1 and 2:11 already imply (Uz, Teman, Shuah, Naamah — all eastern / Edomite / Aramean peripheries).

Closing of the book. Vayyamot Iyov, zaqen u-s'va yamim. The book ends with the standard Hebrew Bible patriarchal-good-death formula and no editorial coda. The narrative is complete.

Contents

  • 1 Job 1 — The bnei-elohim council and the first test
  • 2 Job 2 — The second council scene and the second test
  • 38 Job 38 — YHWH from the whirlwind: the cosmological challenge

  • 41 Job 41 — Leviathan, king over all the proud

Back · Library

Contents

    Wheel of Heaven is an open, multilingual reading of the ancient world's creation traditions — Raëlian, biblical, Mesopotamian, and others — through a single working hypothesis: that the beings called Elohim were a small advanced human civilization that came here, designed life on Earth, and left a record in the religious texts. The reading runs across twelve ages of the precessional cycle, from the project's beginning twenty-two thousand years ago to the present age of disclosure and into the long future the next Great Year is opening. The site is free, source-aware, and built so the work can be checked.
